Accomack.description: The Accomack, also spelled Accawmack or Accomac, were an Algonquian-speaking Indigenous people of the southern Eastern Shore of Virginia, especially in present-day Northampton County. Their coastal homeland placed them near Chesapeake Bay, barrier islands, marshes and tidal waterways. In the early 1600s, the Accomack developed trade and diplomatic relationships with English settlers while maintaining their own leadership and communities. Descendants of the Accomack continue to live on the Eastern Shore and work to preserve their history and cultural identity.
Accomack.language: The Accomack spoke an Algonquian language related to other Native languages of coastal Virginia and the Chesapeake region. Like many Indigenous languages in the area, much of the language was not preserved in written records.
Accomack.culture: As a coastal people, the Accomack relied heavily on fishing, hunting, shellfish gathering and foraging from the marshes, forests and waterways around them. Their location on the Eastern Shore connected them to trade routes across Chesapeake Bay and nearby coastal communities. While some neighboring Native groups practiced more intensive agriculture, the Accomack are especially associated with coastal lifeways shaped by fish, game, wild plants and seasonal resources. Today, Accomack history remains tied to the land and waters of Virginia’s Eastern Shore.

Acolapissa.description: The Acolapissa were a small tribe of Native Americans of North America, who lived in the Southeast of what is the present-day United States. They lived along the banks of the Pearl River, between present-day Louisiana and Mississippi. The word Acolapissa (okla pisa) means "those who look out for people" in the Choctaw language, one of the Muskogean languages. The Acolapissa had at least six villages. Pierre Le Moyne d'Iberville claimed that the Tangipahoa settlement was an additional Acolapissan settlement. In 1699, a band of 200 Chickasaw, led by two English slave traders, attacked several Acolapissa villages, intending to take captives as slaves to be sold in Charleston, South Carolina. Around 1702 the Acolapissa moved from Pearl River and settled on a bayou on the north side of Lake Pontchartrain. Shortly afterward, Louis Juchereau de St. Denis sent the Natchitoches tribe to live with the Acolapissa, who welcomed them and allowed them to settle close to their own village. After that time, in the year 1722 they moved farther west, into the area around the future New Orleans along the Mississippi River. Pressured by French settlement in the area and suffering high mortality from new infectious diseases carried by the Europeans, the Acolapissa tribe eventually merged with the Bayogoula. By the year 1739 these remnants were absorbed into the Houma people and ceased to exist separately as tribes. Their descendants intermarried.
Acolapissa.language: They are believed to have spoken a Muskogean language, closely related to the Choctaw and Chickasaw spoken by other Southeast tribes of the Muskogean family.
Acolapissa.culture: The Acolapissa adorned their bodies with tattoos. Given the warm and humid climate, they wore very little clothing. They built dwellings from local resources, with reed and thatch roofs. Some sources indicate that the Acolapissa may have been the same tribe as the Quinipissa or the Tangipahoa. According to several sources related to the Houma, several tribes in the area of Lake Pontchartrain were called Mougoulacha.

Adena Culture.description: The Adena culture refers to ancient Indigenous communities that lived from about 500 BCE to 100 CE, centered in what is now southern Ohio. Related Adena-style sites have also been found in parts of Indiana, Kentucky, West Virginia and possibly Pennsylvania. The name “Adena” comes from an estate near Chillicothe, Ohio, where early burial mounds of this culture were identified. Adena communities are best known for their earthen mounds, village life and long-distance trade connections.
Adena Culture.language: The language or languages spoken by Adena communities were not recorded. Because the Adena lived long before written records in the region, their identity is understood mainly through archaeology rather than documented language.
Adena Culture.culture: Adena people often lived in small villages with circular houses made from poles, bark and other natural materials. They hunted, fished and gathered wild plants, and they used tools such as stone hoes, axes, projectile points, smoking pipes and simple pottery. Adena communities are especially known for burial mounds and ceremonial earthworks. Ornaments made from copper, mica and seashells show that they traded with distant peoples and were connected to wider Indigenous exchange networks across eastern North America.

Ais.description: The Ais were one of many tribes, consisting of several hundred thousand people, that lived in Florida prior to first contact with Ponce de Leon and the Spanish in 1513. Ais lived in what is now Brevard, Indian River, St. Lucie and northernmost Martin counties. Many lived along the shores of the great lagoon called Rio de Ais now known as the Indian River. We know that they lived in small villages, but we don't know much about their origins or their languages. Like the Calusa, the Ais were devastated by European diseases. Some of the survivors were probably sent to Cuba with the Calusas and Tequestas, while others may have merged with other Floridian Indians and eventually joined the Seminole tribe. Jonathan Dickinson made multiple observations on the appearance, diet and customs of the Ais at the end of the 17th Century and these were found in Jonathan Dickinson's Journal. The Ais did not survive long after Dickinson's recount of the time he spent with them. Shortly after 1700 settlers in Carolina started raiding the Ais to capture slaves. By 1743, when the Spanish established a mission among them, the Ais numbers were declining due to slave raids, disease and rum. The Ais were gone from the area by 1760.
Ais.language: Their language was never recorded and some have guessed that it may have been related to Calusa, based on stories that people from the two tribes could communicate with each other.
Ais.culture: The Ais or Ays as they are sometimes referred as, were hunters and gathers, not farmers. This Native-American tribe gathered fruits and nuts including sea grapes, coco plums, sea oats and palm berries. The Ais tribes fished using hooks made from the toe bones of deer they had hunted, taking full advantage of the great catches available off the coast of Florida. They hunted large game including deer and bears, along with whatever smaller game they could find.

Alabama.description: The Alabama or Alibamu (Alabama: Albaamaha) are a Southeastern culture people of Native Americans, originally from Alabama. They were members of the Muscogee Creek Confederacy, a loose trade and military organization of autonomous towns; their home lands were on the upper Alabama River. The Alabama and closely allied Coushatta people migrated from Alabama and Mississippi to the area of Texas in the late 18th century and early 19th century, under pressure from European-American settlers to the east. They essentially merged and shared reservation land. Although the tribe was terminated in the 1950s, it achieved federal recognition in 1987 as the Alabama-Coushatta Tribe of Texas. Its 1,137 members have about 4,500 acres (18 km2) of reservation.
Alabama.language: The Alabama language is part of the Muskogean language family. Alabama is closely related to Koasati and distantly to Hitchiti, Chickasaw and Choctaw. Also known as Alibamu, an estimated 100 speakers, primarily from Texas, still speak the language.
Alabama.culture: Alabama men were hunters and sometimes fought wars to defend their territory. Alabama women were farmers and also did most of the child care and cooking. Both genders took part in storytelling, artwork and music, and traditional medicine. In the past, the chief was always a man, but today an Alabama Indian woman can participate in government also. The Alabama people lived in square-shaped villages of houses and small farm plots. Alabama houses had plaster and rivercane walls with thatched roofs. These dwellings were about as strong and warm as a log cabin. The Alabamas also built a larger circular lodge for town meetings, and most villages had a lacrosse field with benches for spectators. An Alabama village was usually surrounded with palisades to guard against attack.

Apalachees.description: Prior to European contact, there were at least 50,000-60,000 Apalachees. They were a strong and powerful tribe living in widely dispersed villages. Other tribes respected the Apalachees because they belonged to an advanced Indian civilization, they were prosperous, and they were fierce warriors. The Apalachee were an Indigenous people of the Southeastern Woodlands, specifically an Indigenous people of Florida, who lived in the Florida Panhandle until the early 18th century. They lived between the Aucilla River and Ochlockonee River, at the head of Apalachee Bay, an area known as the Apalachee Province. Following a series of devastating attacks on Spanish Florida by the British and their Creek Indian allies, Mission San Luis was burned and abandoned by its residents on July 31, 1704. Some Apalachees, who were not killed outright or enslaved, migrated north in to Creek territory. Others moved east to St. Augustine with the Spaniards or temporarily relocated in Timucua Province before eventually resettling in St. Augustine.
Apalachees.language: Apalachee was a Muskogean language of the American Southeast, once spoken in the Florida Panhandle and parts of Southeast Georgia. Very little is known of it today. Early Spanish transcripts of the language suggest that it was most closely related to Hitchiti and Alabama.
Apalachees.culture: For food, they grew corn, beans and squash. Men prepared the fields and women tended the crops. Men also hunted bear, deer and small game, while women gathered nuts and berries. Traditionally the men wore deerskin loincloths and women wore Spanish moss skirts. When preparing for battle, the men painted their bodies with red ochre and put feathers in their hair. The Apalachees played a ball game that was a religious exercise as well as a sport. One village would challenge another to a match, and the two teams would have up to 100 players each. They played the ball game in the spring and summer, and dedicated it to the gods of rain and thunder to ensure rain for their crops.

Appamatuck.description:
The Appamatuck, also recorded as Appamattuck, Appomattoc or Appomattox, were an Indigenous people of present-day southeastern Virginia. Their communities were located along the lower Appomattox River, near what are now Petersburg, Colonial Heights and Hopewell. The river’s modern name comes from the Appamatuck people. By the time English colonists arrived at Jamestown in 1607, the Appamatuck were part of the Powhatan paramount chiefdom, a political alliance of Algonquian-speaking communities led by Wahunsenacawh, commonly known as Powhatan. One Appamatuck town was led by Opossunoquonuske, a female leader, or "weroansqua", whom English colonists encountered during early journeys along the river. Another Appamatuck community was led by Coquonasum. Early English accounts describe Appamatuck settlements surrounded by cultivated cornfields and connected to the rivers that supported food, travel and trade.

Relations between the Appamatuck and English colonists changed rapidly as settlers claimed Native land and demanded food. During the First Anglo-Powhatan War, English forces attacked and burned an Appamatuck town in 1611. Continued warfare, land seizure, colonial expansion and population loss forced the Appamatuck from portions of their homeland. After the English defeat of the Powhatan chiefdom in the 1640s, remaining Native communities in eastern Virginia were placed under increasingly restrictive colonial control. Records from the late seventeenth century still identify Appamatuck leadership and community presence, but by the early eighteenth century the Appamatuck were no longer regularly documented as a separate tribal community. Some descendants may have joined other Virginia Indian communities or remained in the region as colonial authorities increasingly erased Native identities from official records.
Appamatuck.language: The Appamatuck spoke an Eastern Algonquian language related to the languages spoken by other peoples of the Powhatan paramount chiefdom. These related languages allowed communities throughout the Virginia Coastal Plain to communicate, trade and participate in shared political relationships. As English settlement expanded, Indigenous languages in Virginia declined because of displacement, colonial pressure and the disruption of Native communities. By 1727, Virginia stopped employing official Powhatan-language interpreters because the language was no longer widely spoken in colonial affairs.
Appamatuck.culture: The Appamatuck lived in riverside communities where the Appomattox River provided fish, transportation routes and access to neighboring villages and trade networks. Like other Powhatan peoples of the region, they grew crops such as corn, gathered wild plants and relied on hunting and fishing for food. Their homes and fields were part of a landscape shaped by waterways, marshlands and fertile soil. The Appamatuck were governed by local leaders called "weroances" or "weroansquas", who served under Powhatan’s broader authority. Opossunoquonuske, the female leader encountered by the English in 1607, demonstrates that women could hold important political leadership roles in Appamatuck and Powhatan society. Although the Appamatuck were displaced as a distinct recorded community during the colonial period, their homeland along the Appomattox River remains part of Virginia’s Indigenous history.

Arrohattec.description: The Arrohattec were an Indigenous people who lived along the James River in what is now central Virginia. Their main settlement, known as Arrohateck Town, was located about twelve miles below the falls at Richmond, near the site where English colonists later established Henricus. The Arrohattec were among the original communities within the Powhatan chiefdom, which was led by Wahunsenacawh, commonly known as Powhatan. By the early seventeenth century, the Powhatan chiefdom included numerous Algonquian-speaking communities across eastern Virginia. In 1607, English explorers John Smith and Christopher Newport visited the Arrohattec community during their travels along the James River. The Arrohattec leader, Ashuaquid, welcomed the English visitors. Historical records describe Ashuaquid as the leader of a town with about sixty warriors. The Arrohattec also assisted the English settlers after an attack on Jamestown later that year. However, relations between Native communities and the English colonists soon worsened as settlers demanded food, claimed land and expanded their settlements. By 1609, the Arrohattec were reportedly reluctant to trade with the colonists. Their population declined during a period marked by conflict, food shortages and likely exposure to European diseases. The last recorded English reference to the Arrohattec as a separate community appeared around 1610. By 1611, their town had been abandoned or displaced when Sir Thomas Dale established the English settlement of Henricus on or near their homeland.
Arrohattec.language: The Arrohattec spoke an Eastern Algonquian language related to the languages spoken by other communities within the Powhatan chiefdom. This shared language helped connect Native communities throughout eastern Virginia through trade, diplomacy, kinship and political alliances. The English recorded some names and titles, including "weroance", meaning a male leader, and "weroansqua," meaning a female leader, but much of the Arrohattec language was not preserved in written records.
Arrohattec.culture: The Arrohattec lived in a riverside community where the James River provided fish, freshwater resources, transportation and connections to neighboring settlements. Like other Powhatan peoples of eastern Virginia, they combined farming with fishing, hunting and gathering. They likely grew crops such as corn, beans and squash; gathered available plants and nuts; and hunted deer and smaller animals in the surrounding forests. Their homes, sometimes called "yehakins," were built using bent saplings covered with woven mats or bark. Canoes made travel, fishing and communication along the James River possible. The Arrohattec were governed by a local leader, or "weroance," under the broader authority of Powhatan. Although they were no longer documented as a separate community after the early colonial period, the site of Arrohateck Town and the James River landscape remain important parts of Virginia’s Indigenous history.

Avoyel.description: The Avoyel people were a small tribe Natchez-speaking band who called themselves Tamoucougoula. The word Avoyel is of French derivation and means either “Flint People” or “the people of the rocks,” reflecting their active trading of flint for tools. The Avoyel or Avoyelles were a small Native American tribe who at the time of European contact inhabited land near the mouth of the Red River at its confluence with the Atchafalaya River near present-day Marksville, Louisiana. At the time of European contact they lived in a number of villages on the Red River in locations near present-day Alexandria and a palisaded village near Marksville. They controlled the river to its confluence with the lower Black River, Upper Atchafalaya River and the Mississippi.
Avoyel.language: The Avoyel language may have been related to the Natchez language.
Avoyel.culture: Living in villages, they cultivated maize and vegetables and practiced the arts common to the tribes of the Gulf region. Like their neighbors, they had come into possession of horses, which they bred, and later they obtained cattle. During the general displacement of the tribes throughout the Gulf states, which began in the 18th century, the Avoyel country proved to be attractive. When the French made contact with the Avoyel tribe, it was recorded that the tribe numbered 280 people, which would make them one of the smallest tribes to make contact with the Europeans

Biloxi.description: The Biloxi, also known as the "Tanêks" people, are an Indigenous people originally associated with the Gulf Coast of present-day Mississippi. When French explorers first recorded contact with them in 1699, Biloxi communities lived in the Pascagoula River region near the Mississippi coast. The name of the present-day city of Biloxi comes from the Biloxi people. Some evidence suggests that Spanish explorers may have encountered Biloxi ancestors in the southeastern interior more than a century earlier, but the 1699 French encounter is the first well-documented contact with people identified as Biloxi. European colonization disrupted Biloxi communities through disease, land loss, trade pressures and changing political alliances. During the eighteenth century, many Biloxi moved westward into Louisiana. After 1779, Biloxi and Ofo allies joined the Tunica in settling on the Avoyelles Prairie near present-day Marksville, Louisiana. Over time, the historic Biloxi, Tunica, Ofo and Avoyel peoples became closely connected through shared communities, intermarriage and the pressures of colonial and United States expansion. In 1924, Biloxi representatives formally authorized a union with the Tunica under the leadership of Eli Barbry. Today, Biloxi descendants are part of the federally recognized "Tunica-Biloxi Tribe of Louisiana", whose reservation and tribal lands are located in Avoyelles and Rapides parishes in east-central Louisiana. The tribe gained federal recognition in 1981.
Biloxi.language: The Biloxi spoke a Siouan language, which made them culturally and linguistically distinct from many neighboring Gulf Coast peoples who spoke Muskogean languages. Biloxi was related to the language of the Ofo people, who later also became part of the modern Tunica-Biloxi community. Linguists recorded parts of the Biloxi language during the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, but far less Biloxi-language material survives than documentation of the Tunica language. Today, the Tunica-Biloxi Tribe’s language revitalization efforts have focused primarily on reclaiming and teaching Tunica through classes, workshops, children’s stories, songs and cultural programs.
Biloxi.culture: The Biloxi lived in communities shaped by the rivers, wetlands and coastal resources of the Mississippi Gulf Coast. They depended on fishing, gathering shellfish, hunting and farming. Historical accounts describe Gulf Coast Native communities growing corn and other crops, hunting animals such as deer, bear and buffalo, and gathering food from the waterways and forests around them. Biloxi families lived in long houses made with mud walls and bark roofs, and they made useful items including pottery, baskets, wooden bowls and tools from bone and horn. The Biloxi followed a matrilineal kinship system, meaning family relationships and social identity were traced through women’s family lines. Biloxi culture continues through the Tunica-Biloxi Tribe of Louisiana. Tribal members may identify as Tunica, Biloxi or Biloxi-Choctaw, even though many families share ancestry from multiple tribal communities. The tribe preserves its heritage through its Cultural and Educational Resources Center, annual pow wow, traditional songs and stories, crafts, dance, history education and language reclamation programs. These efforts allow Biloxi descendants and the broader Tunica-Biloxi community to pass cultural knowledge to younger generations while continuing as a sovereign tribal nation.

Caddo.description: The Caddo people comprise the Caddo Nation of Oklahoma, a federally recognized tribe headquartered in Binger, Oklahoma. The Caddo Confederacy was a network of Indigenous peoples of the Southeastern Woodlands, who historically inhabited much of what is northeast Texas, west Louisiana, southwestern Arkansas, and southeastern Oklahoma. Prior to European contact, they were the Caddoan Mississippian culture, who constructed huge earthwork mounds at several sites in this territory, flourishing about 800 to 1400 CE. In the early 19th century, Caddo people were forced to a reservation in Texas. In 1859, they were removed to Indian Territory. Twentieth-century archeological investigations of many prehistoric Caddoan sites indicate that Caddo communities were widely dispersed throughout the major and minor stream valleys of the Caddoan area by around A.D. 800.
Caddo.language: They speak the Caddo language and is critically endangered, with no exclusively Caddo-speaking community and as of 2023 only 2 speakers who acquired the language as children outside school instruction, down from 25 speakers in 1997. Caddo has several mutually intelligible dialects. The most commonly used dialects are Hasinai and Hainai; others include Kadohadacho, Natchitoches and Yatasi.
Caddo.culture: The Caddo were a semisedentary agricultural people living in conical dwellings of poles covered with a thatch of grass and grouped around ceremonial temple mounds. They were skillful potters and basket makers, wove cloth of vegetable fibres and, on special occasions, wore mantles decorated with feathers. They also wore nose rings and, like many other southeastern tribes, adorned their bodies with tattoos. Traditional Caddo descent was matrilineal, and a hereditary upper group, marked by head flattening and other status symbols, directed political and religious activities. There are scattered reports of ceremonial human sacrifice and cannibalism; these and other traits probably indicate trade or other links between the Caddo and the centres of Aztec or Mayan cultures in Mexico and Yucatan.

Calusa.description: The Calusa were a Native American people of Florida's southwest coast. Calusa society developed from that of archaic peoples of the Everglades region. Previous indigenous cultures had lived in the area for thousands of years. At the time of European contact in the 16th and 17th centuries, the historic Calusa were the people of the Caloosahatchee culture. They developed a complex culture based on estuarine fisheries rather than agriculture. Calusa territory reached from Charlotte Harbor to Cape Sable, all of present-day Charlotte, Lee, and Collier counties, and may have included the Florida Keys at times. They had the highest population density of South Florida; estimates of total population at the time of European contact range from 10,000 to several times that, but these are speculative.
Calusa.language: Calusa is an extinct Amerindian language of Florida. It is based on the Mvskoke and Mikasuki (languages of the present-day Seminole and Miccosukee nations) ethnonym for the people who had lived around the Caloosahatchee River.
Calusa.culture: Hernando de Escalante Fontaneda, a Spaniard held captive in the 16th century, recorded that Calusa meant "fierce people" in their language. By the early 19th century, Anglo-Americans in the area used the term Calusa for the people. The Calusa diet at settlements along the coast and estuaries consisted primarily of fish, pigfish, and hardhead catfish. These small fish were supplemented by larger bony fish, sharks and rays, mollusks, crustaceans, ducks, sea turtles and land turtles, and land animals. The Calusa caught most of their fish with nets woven with a standard mesh size. The Calusa made bone and shell gauges that they used in net weaving. Cultivated gourds were used as floats, and sinkers and weights were made from mollusk shells. The Calusa also used spears, hooks, and throat gorges to catch fish. Well-preserved nets, net floats, and hooks were found at Key Marco, in the territory of the neighboring Muspa tribe.

Cape Fear.description: The Cape Fear Indians were a small, coastal tribe of Native Americans who lived on the Cape Fear River in North Carolina (now Carolina Beach State Park). The autonym of the Cape Fear Indians may have been Daw-hee. Their name for the area was Chicora. Of their villages, only one, Necoes, is known by name. Smallpox spread from Spanish colonies in Florida to the Carolinas in the 16th century. The population of the Cape Fear Indians was estimated to be 1,000 in 1600. A colonial census in 1715 recorded that they numbered 206. In 1664 the settlement called Charles Towne was founded, but it was abandoned in 1667 after war broke out between the Cape Fear Indians and the settlers over British slavery of Indians.
Cape Fear.language: Their language is unknown and may have been a Siouan language.
Cape Fear.culture: The Cape Fear Indians and the Winyaw migrated from their coastal villages up the Pee Dee River adjacent to a trading post the British founded in 1716. They eventually settled inland from Charleston in what is now known as Williamsburg County, South Carolina. In May 1749 provision was made by the Governor in Council to render protection to them through supplying their representatives with fifty pounds of bullets and twenty-five pounds of powder. There had previously been complaints that the Cape Fear were being abused and driven from nearby hunting lands by neighboring Europeans. It was ruled that they were a peaceable people and that their rights to hunt were protected by the government.

Chahta Yakni (Choctaw).description: The Choctaw (in the Choctaw language, Chahta) are a Native American people originally based in the Southeastern Woodlands, in what is now Alabama and Mississippi. Their Choctaw language is a Western Muskogean language. Today, Choctaw people are enrolled in three federally recognized tribes: the Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma, Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians, and Jena Band of Choctaw Indians in Louisiana. The Choctaw were first noted by Europeans in French written records of 1675. Their mother mound is Nanih Waiya, a great earthwork platform mound located in central-east Mississippi. Early Spanish explorers of the mid-16th century in the Southeast encountered ancestral Mississippian culture villages and chiefs.
Chahta Yakni (Choctaw).language: The Choctaw language, spoken by the Choctaw, an Indigenous people of the Southeastern Woodlands, USA, is a member of the Muskogean language family. Chickasaw is a separate but closely related language to Choctaw.
Chahta Yakni (Choctaw).culture: The Choctaw people are believed to have consolidated in the 17th century, perhaps from peoples from Alabama and the Plaquemine culture. The Choctaw practiced head flattening as a ritual adornment for its people, but the practice eventually fell out of favor. Some of their communities had extensive trade and interaction with Europeans, including people from Spain, France, and England greatly shaped it as well. After the United States was formed and its settlers began to move into the Southeast, the Choctaw were among the Five Civilized Tribes, who adopted some of their ways. They transitioned to yeoman farming methods, and accepted European Americans and African Americans into their society. In mid-summer the Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians celebrate their traditional culture during the Choctaw Indian Fair with ball games, dancing, cooking and entertainment.

Chakchiuma.description: The Chakchiuma were a Native American tribe of the upper Yazoo River region of what is today the state of Mississippi. The identification of the Chakchiuma by the French of the late 17th century as "a Chicacha nation" indicates that they were related to the Chickasaw and of similar Western Muskogean stock. They likely shared a common origin as the Chickasaw and Choctaw people and were absorbed into the Chickasaw Nation in the mid-18th century. The first historical reference to the Chakchiuma is found when Hernando de Soto sent a contingent of troops against them while he was staying with the Chickasaw. In 1700, English colonists from the Province of Carolina convinced Quapaw tribesmen to attack the Chakchiuma in order to capture members of their tribe to sell into slavery in the Carolinas; the ensuing attack was unsuccessful. The Chakchiuma participated on the French side in the Yazoo War. In about 1739 the Chakchiuma were involved in hostilities, primarily with the Chickasaw, that led to their destruction as an independent tribe and their being incorporated into the Chickasaw and Choctaw tribes.
Chakchiuma.language: The Choctaw language is a member of the Muskogean family and was well known among the frontiersmen, such as Andrew Jackson and William Henry Harrison, of the early 19th century. The language is closely related to Chickasaw, and some linguists consider the two as dialects of a single language.
Chakchiuma.culture: The Chakchiuma have also been claimed to be the ancestors of the Houma tribe, who have a red crawfish as their war totem, though their existence as distinct groups at least from their first contact with Europeans which was confirmed by French encounters. in 1699 they were probably the most populous of the Yazoo tribes, and spoke the Chickasaw language. They were an important tribe at the time of De Soto’s expedition (1540-41) and lived in a walled town. During the 18th century they were included in the Chickasaw confederacy, and had the reputation of being warlike. hey transitioned to yeoman farming methods, and accepted European Americans and African Americans into their society. In mid-summer the Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians celebrate their traditional culture during the Choctaw Indian Fair with ball games, dancing, cooking and entertainment.

Chatot.description: The Chatot (also Chacato or Chactoo) were a Native American tribe who lived in the upper Apalachicola River and Chipola River basins in what is now Florida. They spoke a Muskogean language, which may have been the same as that of several other peoples in western Florida, including the Amacano, Chine, Pacara, and Pensacola. Patricia Galloway, and were connected with the Choctaw. The Chatot were involved in a war with the Apalachee and Amacano people in 1639. When the Chato were first discovered west of the Apalachicola River they were known as the Chacatos, Chaqtos, Chatots and the Chactots; after numerous relocations of the tribe new synonyms referring to the names of the Chato People were generated after their sighting in 1763. These synonymous names included the Chactoo, Chacchous, Chaetoos and Chattoos.
Chatot.language: They spoke a Muskogean language, which may have been the same as that of several other peoples in western Florida.
Chatot.culture: The culture and nature of the Chato are largely unknown due to the lack of recorded history on their way of life. Based on the proximal location of the Chato to the neighboring Apalachee Tribe, it is inferred that the Chato’s culture and way of living were similar to that of the Apalachee. In 1675 the Chato executed a religious revolt against the missionaries due to the suffocating nature of the Spaniards attempting to impress the Christian religion upon Chato beliefs. The conflict did not last long due to another neighboring tribe, the Chisca, that continually raided the missionary villages.

Cheraw.description: The Cheraw people, also known as the Saraw or Saura, of Indigenous people of the Southeastern Woodlands, in the Piedmont area of North Carolina near the Sauratown Mountains, east of Pilot Mountain and north of the Yadkin River. They lived in villages near the Catawba River. Their first European and African contact was with the Hernando De Soto Expedition in 1540. The early explorer John Lawson included them in the larger eastern-Siouan confederacy, which he called "the Esaw Nation." After attacks in the late 17th century and early 18th century, they moved to the southeast around the Pee Dee River, where the Cheraw name became more widely used. They became extinct as a tribe, although some descendants survived as remnant peoples. Though they were once almost as large as the Tuscarora tribe in North Carolina, they are less prominent in history because they were almost completely gone before the white settlements had reached their territory.
Cheraw.language: The Cheraw people, also known as the Saraw or Saura, were a Siouan-speaking tribe of Indigenous people of the Southeastern Woodlands.
Cheraw.culture: Originally known as the "Saraw", they became known by the name of one of their villages, Cherae. The name they called themselves is lost to history, but the Cherokee called them Ani-Suwa'li. Little is known about the Cheraw history prior to encountering the Europeans in 1534. The Men wore loin cloth made of deerskin, the women knee-length skirts of deerskin and also the tribe wore pants, leggings, and capes made of various animal hides were worn in the winter and for travel by both men and women. The homes were made of saplings covered with bark and animal skins and the villages were usually formed along a river, consisting of groups of homes and in some cases a ceremonial building and plaza in a central location. there were adapt at farming beans, corn, and squash and hunting various wild game, mostly on deer and bear.

Cheroenhaka (Nottoway).description: The Cheroenhaka, commonly called the Nottoway in English colonial records, are an Indigenous people of southeastern Virginia. According to tribal history, the name "Cheroenhaka" means “People at the Fork of the Stream,” referring to their homeland near the place where the Nottoway and Blackwater rivers join to form the Chowan River. Their communities extended through areas that are now Nottoway, Sussex and Southampton counties in Virginia. Archaeological findings at the Hand Site in Southampton County document an Iroquoian settlement and burial ground associated with Cheroenhaka ancestors dating to around 1580. The Cheroenhaka first appear in English records in 1608, when Nathaniel Powell and Anas Todkill traveled to a village called Tomihitton while searching for information about survivors of the Roanoke Colony. Early English and Algonquian-speaking sources referred to the tribe as "Mangoak", "Mengwe" or "Nottoway", rather than by their own name. In 1650, the Bland-Wood expedition encountered Cheroenhaka towns along the Nottoway River, including Chounteroute Town and Tonnatorah. During the seventeenth century, war, colonial settlement, land encroachment and disease greatly reduced the tribe’s population and restricted access to hunting and living areas. The Cheroenhaka signed treaties with Virginia colonial authorities in 1646, 1677 and 1713. After the Treaty of Middle Plantation in 1677, continued colonial settlement pressured the tribe to move farther southeast along the Nottoway River. In 1705, the colonial government established two reservation tracts for the Cheroenhaka, called the Circle and Square tracts, totaling about 41,000 acres in what are now Southampton and Sussex counties. The tribe’s 1713 treaty with Lieutenant Governor Alexander Spotswood addressed land, schooling, military obligations and colonial control of tribal affairs. Over the following centuries, most reservation land passed out of Cheroenhaka ownership; by 1877, only 575 acres remained to be divided among five Cheroenhaka families. Cheroenhaka families remained in Southampton County, including in the community historically known as Artis Town. In 2002, descendants reorganized a modern tribal government and renewed public cultural gatherings. In 2009, the tribe purchased 100 acres of former reservation land near Courtland, Virginia, where it established "Cattashowrock Town" as a center for cultural activities and annual Green Corn and Corn Harvest Powwows.
Cheroenhaka (Nottoway).language: The Cheroenhaka spoke an Iroquoian language related to the languages of the Meherrin and Tuscarora peoples. This made them linguistically different from the neighboring Powhatan communities, who spoke Eastern Algonquian languages. In 1820, John Wood recorded words from "Edie Turner", also known as Wanè Rounseraw", a Cheroenhaka woman living on the tribe’s Southampton County reservation. Thomas Jefferson sent a copy of this vocabulary to linguist Peter Du Ponceau, who identified the language as Iroquoian. A second vocabulary was recorded in the 1830s by James Trezevant, who also documented the name Cheroenhaka as the tribe’s name for itself.
Cheroenhaka (Nottoway).culture: The Cheroenhaka lived along rivers, creeks and swamps that supported farming, fishing, hunting, travel and trade. Historical accounts describe Cheroenhaka towns protected by wooden palisades and containing bark-covered longhouses. When Alexander Spotswood visited the tribe’s town in 1711, he described a fortified settlement with homes and gardens where the people grew corn, beans, squash and tobacco. The Nottoway, Blackwater and Chowan rivers were central to Cheroenhaka life, providing fish, transportation and access to hunting and gathering areas. Cheroenhaka social and political life included hereditary leaders, called chiefs or "teerheers" in the attached tribal history. The tribe participated in diplomacy with colonial governments and other Native nations, including the exchange of a peace belt with the Cherokee in Williamsburg in 1751. Cheroenhaka men also served alongside Virginia forces during the French and Indian War. Despite land loss and efforts by Virginia authorities to classify Native families under racial categories other than Indian, Cheroenhaka descendants continued practicing traditions such as hunting, trapping, fishing, farming, hide tanning and community gathering. Today, the tribe maintains cultural traditions through powwows, educational programs, stewardship of Cattashowrock Town and the preservation of its history and ancestral homeland in Southampton County.

Cherokee (Oklahoma).description:
The Cherokee people, who call themselves "Aniyvwiya" or "Anigiduwagi", originally lived across a broad region of the southeastern United States, including areas of present-day Alabama, Georgia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Virginia, Kentucky and West Virginia. By the early nineteenth century, after decades of land cessions forced through treaties and colonial pressure, the Cherokee Nation’s remaining eastern homeland was centered in parts of Georgia, Tennessee, Alabama and North Carolina. The Cherokee Nation developed a constitutional government and, in 1827, adopted a written constitution. When the state of Georgia attempted to take Cherokee lands and abolish Cherokee governmental authority, the Cherokee Nation challenged those actions in the United States Supreme Court. In "Worcester v. Georgia" in 1832, the Court ruled that Georgia did not have authority to impose its laws within the Cherokee Nation. Despite Cherokee resistance, a small unauthorized group signed the Treaty of New Echota in 1835, surrendering the Nation’s eastern lands in exchange for territory in Indian Territory, present-day Oklahoma. The majority of Cherokee people opposed the treaty and refused to leave their homelands. In 1838, the United States military forcibly removed approximately 16,000 Cherokee people westward on the journey remembered as the "Trail of Tears". About 4,000 Cherokee died in removal camps or along the route from disease, starvation, exposure and harsh travel conditions. After arrival in Indian Territory, Cherokee people rebuilt their government at Tahlequah and adopted a new constitution in 1839.

Today, the Cherokee Nation is a federally recognized sovereign tribal government headquartered in Tahlequah, Oklahoma. It is one of three federally recognized Cherokee tribes and has more than 450,000 citizens worldwide. The Cherokee Nation exercises jurisdiction over its reservation in northeastern Oklahoma, an area encompassing all or parts of 14 counties. Through its government, services and cultural programs, the Nation continues to protect Cherokee sovereignty, community life, language and traditions for future generations.
Cherokee (Oklahoma).language: The Cherokee language, called "Tsalagi" or "ᏣᎳᎩ", is an Iroquoian language. It is related to languages spoken by peoples farther north, rather than to the Muskogean and Siouan languages spoken by many neighboring peoples in the Southeast. In the early nineteenth century, Sequoyah created a writing system for the Cherokee language known as the "Cherokee syllabary." Instead of letters representing individual sounds as in the English alphabet, the syllabary uses 85 characters representing spoken syllables. Cherokee people quickly adopted the syllabary, and the "Cherokee Phoenix," first published in 1828, printed articles in both Cherokee and English. The Cherokee language remains central to Cherokee Nation identity and cultural survival. The Cherokee Nation works to preserve and revitalize the language through classes, digital resources, community programs and the Cherokee Immersion School, where students learn through spoken and written Cherokee using Sequoyah’s syllabary.
Cherokee (Oklahoma).culture: Before removal, Cherokee people lived in towns throughout the mountains, river valleys and woodlands of the Southeast. Cherokee communities relied on farming, hunting, fishing and gathering. They grew crops including corn, beans and squash, while hunting deer, turkey and other animals and gathering foods and medicines from surrounding forests and waterways. Cherokee towns included homes, council houses and public gathering spaces where people made political decisions, conducted ceremonies and maintained relationships among families and communities. Cherokee society traditionally traced clan membership through the mother’s family. The seven Cherokee clans helped organize family relationships, responsibilities and community belonging. Cherokee political life also included town leadership and councils, traditions that continued as the Cherokee Nation developed written constitutional governments in the nineteenth century. After forced removal, Cherokee people rebuilt homes, farms, schools, churches, businesses and government institutions in Indian Territory. They also established the "Cherokee Advocate" newspaper in Tahlequah in 1844, publishing in both Cherokee and English. Cherokee culture continues in Oklahoma through language preservation, storytelling, traditional foods, art, basketry, pottery, music, dance, community gatherings and the ongoing work of the Cherokee Nation government. Each year, the Cherokee National Holiday commemorates the rebuilding of the Cherokee government after the Trail of Tears and honors the strength of Cherokee ancestors and citizens. Through these traditions and institutions, the Cherokee Nation remains a living sovereign nation rather than only a people of the past.

Chesapeake.description: The Chesepian or Chesapeake were a Native American tribe who lived near present-day South Hampton Roads in the U.S. state of Virginia. They occupied an area which is now the Norfolk County or Princess Anne County. The name Chesapeake is an anglicization of the Algonquian word, K'che-sepi-ack, which translates as "country on a great river." In 1585, their name was recorded by English colonists as Ehesepiooc. Their name is spelled many different ways and also listed as Chesapians. The main village of the Chesepian was on the Lynnhaven River in Princess Anne County. It was named Skicoke, located in the present independent city of Norfolk. There was not one single culture that defined people in this area. Instead, there was a diversity of cultures, languages, political groups, and identities.
Chesapeake.language: Although they spoke an Eastern Algonquian language like many tribes within the Powhatan Confederacy, archaeological evidence suggests that the Chesepian people originally belonged to another group, the Carolina Algonquian.
Chesapeake.culture: Most of the indigenous peoples of the Chesapeake identified their lineage through their mothers, not their fathers. People had several names, including a personal name, a name used when they were a child, and a name taken when they were older. Religion was widely practiced in indigenous cultures. Creation stories explained the origin of the world and the role of humans within it, priests provided advice to leaders and the larger community, and prayer was a part of everyday life. To provide for all of the tribe’s needs, men and women had different responsibilities. Women did the farming, gathering, and housebuilding. Men hunted and defended the tribe in times of conflict. American Indians in the Chesapeake constructed a type of house known as a longhouse. The women of the tribe constructed them by building a wooden frame with an arched roof and covering the frame with mats woven from reeds. The lifestyle as well as ceremonial practices of tribes revolved around the changing seasons.

Chickahominy.description: The Chickahominy are an Indigenous people whose ancestral homeland is along the Chickahominy River in present-day Virginia. Their name has been interpreted as referring to “coarse-ground corn people,” reflecting the importance of corn and farming in their community life. Before English colonization, Chickahominy people lived in permanent villages along the river that still bears their name, between what are now Richmond and Williamsburg. They were one of the Algonquian-speaking Native peoples of Virginia’s Coastal Plain. Although the Chickahominy are often grouped with the Powhatan Indians because of their cultural and linguistic connections, the tribe states that its villages maintained their independence and were not governed by Wahunsenacawh, commonly known as Chief Powhatan. After the English established Jamestown in 1607, Chickahominy people encountered colonists whose expanding settlements placed increasing pressure on Native lands and resources. The Treaty of 1646 displaced the Chickahominy from their river homeland and assigned them land in the Pamunkey Neck area of Virginia. As English settlement continued to expand, the Chickahominy were pushed out of that area as well. Chickahominy families gradually moved to Chickahominy Ridge in present-day Charles City County, near one of their early village sites, where many tribal citizens continue to live today. During the twentieth century, Virginia’s Racial Integrity Act and related policies attempted to erase Native identity by changing or destroying records of Native births, marriages and deaths. Chickahominy children were also denied equal educational opportunities in Virginia, leading tribal families to establish their own schools and, in some cases, send students to Oklahoma to complete high school or attend college. The Chickahominy Tribe was officially recognized by the Commonwealth of Virginia in 1983. On January 29, 2018, the tribe received federal recognition through the Thomasina E. Jordan Indian Tribes of Virginia Federal Recognition Act. Today, the Chickahominy Tribe remains a sovereign Native nation centered in Charles City County, Virginia.
Chickahominy.language: The Chickahominy historically spoke an Eastern Algonquian language related to the languages spoken by other Native peoples of Virginia’s Coastal Plain. The broader term **Algonquian** refers to the language family and cultural relationships shared by many Indigenous nations throughout eastern North America. Although English eventually became the primary language spoken in the Chickahominy community, the tribe continues to preserve its Native identity through history, community traditions, cultural education and its connection to the Chickahominy River homeland.
Chickahominy.culture: The Chickahominy lived in settled riverside villages where families farmed, hunted, fished and gathered plants from the forests, wetlands and waterways around them. Corn was an important crop, along with beans and squash. The Chickahominy River supported fishing, transportation and connections between villages. Children learned from parents, elders and other members of the community, gaining the skills needed for farming, gathering food, making tools, healing, speaking in council and understanding the seasonal cycles of the land. Spirituality was part of daily Chickahominy life. According to tribal history, meals, plants, animals, rivers and the rising sun were understood as gifts from **Ahone**, the Creator. Life in the village depended on respect for the natural world and responsibility to the community. These values remain important to the Chickahominy today, particularly as the tribe emphasizes environmental stewardship and living in balance with creation. In 1901, the Chickahominy established **Samaria Indian Baptist Church**, which became an important center of worship, education and tribal community life. During segregation, when Native students were barred from receiving equal schooling in Virginia, the church and Chickahominy families helped support schools for tribal children. Today, the Chickahominy continue their culture through community gatherings, tribal government, cultural education, environmental programs and the preservation of their ancestral connection to the land and river that carry their name.

Chicora.description: Chicora was a legendary Native American kingdom or tribe sought during the 16th century by various European explorers in present-day South Carolina. The legend originated after Spanish slave traders captured an Indian they called Francisco de Chicora in 1521; afterward, they came to treat Francisco's home country as a land of abundant wealth and natural resources. The "Chicora Legend" influenced both the Spanish and the French in their attempts to colonize North America for the next 60 years. In 1521, Spanish slavers embarked on an expedition to the Southeastern United States. On June 24, they sighted what is thought to be the area around the mouth of the Santee River; they named their discovery the Land of St. John the Baptist. For the next 22 days they explored the river and nearby Winyah Bay and made contact with the locals, including, they claimed, a town or people called Chicora. Ultimately, the slavers compelled around 60 Indians to board their ships and then sailed off without warning, intending to sell the captives into slavery in Hispaniola.
Chicora.language: They had no written language, and likely to have spoken a Siouan language, but that traditions and tales were passed down from father to son
Chicora.culture: The Chicora were traditionally a coastal tribe living near Pawleys Island, South Carolina. They grew corn, tobacco and beans in their gardens and domesticated animals like deer and chickens. Because of their location, the Chicora may have been some of the first Native Americans to see the Spanish explorers arrive in the 1520s. A peaceful tribe, the Chicora traded gifts with the Spanish. But the Spanish explorers to the "New World" had few good intentions. The Chicora people grew corn, tobacco, gourds, squash, and beans in their gardens and domesticated animals like deer and chickens. They fished the rivers and salt water marshes of the area as well as sea fishing.

Chikashsha I̠yaakni’ (Chickasaw).description: The Chickasaw territory was in northern Mississippi, northwestern and northern Alabama, western Tennessee and southwestern Kentucky. Chickasaw people have a migration story in which they moved from a land west of the Mississippi River to reach present-day northeast Mississippi, northwest Alabama, and into Lawrence County, Tennessee. They had interaction with French, English, and Spanish colonists during the colonial period. The United States considered the Chickasaw one of the Five Civilized Tribes of the Southeast, as they adopted numerous practices of European Americans. Resisting European-American settlers encroaching on their territory, they were forced by the U.S. government to sell their traditional lands in the 1832 Treaty of Pontotoc Creek and move to Indian Territory (Oklahoma) during the era of Indian removal in the 1830s.
Chikashsha I̠yaakni’ (Chickasaw).language: Chickasaw is a Muskogean language, and Chickasaw and Choctaw together form the Western branch of the Muskogean language family.
Chikashsha I̠yaakni’ (Chickasaw).culture: The Chickasaw Indians were a tribe of great hunters and warriors whose towns were located near the headwaters of the Tombigbee River in northeastern Mississippi, but who ranged far and wide over the whole Mississippi valley region. The Chickasaw were divided into two moieties, or divisions, which were in turn divided into numerous clans. A person inherited the clan of his mother and was forbidden to marry within that clan. The head chief, or High Minko, was chosen from the Minko clan, and was aided by a council of advisers made up of clan leaders and tribal elders. The supreme deity of the Chickasaw was Ababinili, a composite of the Four Beloved Things Above: Sun, Clouds, Clear Sky and He That Lives in the Clear Sky.

Chincoteague.description: The Chincoteague were an Indigenous people of the Eastern Shore region, associated with present-day Chincoteague Island and nearby coastal areas of Virginia and Maryland. Their homeland included barrier islands, marshes, bays and tidal waterways. Like many Eastern Shore Native communities, they were deeply affected by English settlement, land loss and colonial expansion.
Chincoteague.language: The Chincoteague likely spoke an Eastern Algonquian language related to other Indigenous languages of the Chesapeake and Eastern Shore region.
Chincoteague.culture: The Chincoteague lived in a coastal environment shaped by fishing, shellfish gathering, hunting, trade and seasonal movement. Their communities relied on bays, marshes and barrier islands for food and transportation. Over time, many Chincoteague people were absorbed into neighboring Native communities or disappeared from colonial records as a separate tribe.

Choctaw (Oklahoma).description: The Choctaw (in the Choctaw language, Chahta) are a Native American people originally based in the Southeastern Woodlands, in what is now Alabama and Mississippi. Their Choctaw language is a Western Muskogean language. Today, Choctaw people are enrolled in three federally recognized tribes: the Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma, Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians, and Jena Band of Choctaw Indians in Louisiana. The Choctaw were first noted by Europeans in French written records of 1675. Their mother mound is Nanih Waiya, a great earthwork platform mound located in central-east Mississippi. Early Spanish explorers of the mid-16th century in the Southeast encountered ancestral Mississippian culture villages and chiefs.
Choctaw (Oklahoma).language: The Choctaw language, spoken by the Choctaw, an Indigenous people of the Southeastern Woodlands, USA, is a member of the Muskogean language family. Chickasaw is a separate but closely related language to Choctaw.
Choctaw (Oklahoma).culture: The Choctaw people are believed to have consolidated in the 17th century, perhaps from peoples from Alabama and the Plaquemine culture. The Choctaw practiced head flattening as a ritual adornment for its people, but the practice eventually fell out of favor. Some of their communities had extensive trade and interaction with Europeans, including people from Spain, France, and England greatly shaped it as well. After the United States was formed and its settlers began to move into the Southeast, the Choctaw were among the Five Civilized Tribes, who adopted some of their ways. They transitioned to yeoman farming methods, and accepted European Americans and African Americans into their society. In mid-summer the Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians celebrate their traditional culture during the Choctaw Indian Fair with ball games, dancing, cooking and entertainment.

Chowanoke.description: The Chowanoc (Chowanoke), who historically lived near the Chowan River in North Carolina. At the time of the first English contact in 1580s, they were a large and influential tribe and remained so through the mid-17th century. At the time of Sir Walter Raleigh's colonization efforts from 1584 to 1590, the Chowanoac were probably the most powerful of the Carolina Algonquians. After warfare, in 1677 English colonists set aside a reservation for the tribe near Bennett Creek. The Chowanoc suffered high mortality due to infectious disease, including a smallpox epidemic in 1696. Descendants of the Chowanoc merged with the Tuscarora in the early 18th century. The name Chowanoc has also been spelled Chawanook, Chowanock, Chowanoke, and Chawwonock. They are also known as the Chowanoc Confederacy.[\ Thename is Algonquian and translates as "they of the south" or "southerners".
Chowanoke.language: The Chowanoc, also Chowanoke, are an Algonquian-speaking Native American tribe who historically lived near the Chowan River in North Carolina.
Chowanoke.culture: The Chowanoc had settlements from north of the confluence of the Chowan and Meherrin Rivers to the mouth of the Chowan River. Smaller towns were likely built along Bennett Creek and tributaries of the Meherrin and Wiccacon Rivers. Their villages, farms, and fisheries were bounded by their Iroquoian Mangoak (later Tuscarora) neighbors south and west of Salmon Creek in Bertie County, their Algonquian Weapemeoc neighbors east of Rockyhock Creek in Chowan County, and their Algonquian Nansemond and Iroquoian Nottoway, Meherrin, and Wyanoke neighbors north of the convergence of the Meherrin and Blackwater Rivers on the present North Carolina-Virginia border. The swampy lowlands of these watercourses were shared "hunting quarters" for these various groups of Woodland Indians.

Coharie.description:
The Coharie are a Native American tribe located in southeastern North Carolina, mainly in Sampson and Harnett counties. The Coharie descend from the historic Neusiok Indians and are also connected to other Native peoples of the region, including Tuscarora, Coree and related groups. Their present-day communities are centered near the Big Coharie and Little Coharie rivers, where Coharie families have lived continuously since the eighteenth century. Historical movement caused by intertribal conflict, colonial pressure and White/Indian hostilities brought the Coharie to this area between about 1729 and 1746. Since then, they have maintained a distinct tribal community around the rivers that carry their name. 

The Coharie built a strong political and community presence in Sampson County during the nineteenth century. In 1859, Coharie families established their own subscription school for Coharie children, using their own money and teachers. In 1911, the North Carolina legislature allowed the Coharie to operate their own school system. Although that permission was briefly withdrawn in 1913, it was restored four years later after tribal advocacy. In 1943, the Coharie were given the East Carolina Indian School, a high school that served Coharie students as well as Native students from surrounding counties. The original East Carolina Indian School building later became the Coharie Tribal Administrative Offices. 

The Coharie Tribe has been recognized by the state of North Carolina since 1971. Today, the tribe has more than 3,000 enrolled members. The contemporary Coharie community includes four main settlements: Holly Grove, New Bethel, Shiloh and Antioch. The tribe is governed by the Coharie Intra-Tribal Council, Inc., along with elected tribal leadership. The Coharie continue to provide services in housing, economic development, employment, education, tribal enrollment, cultural arts, health and community support.
Coharie.language: The Coharie are historically connected to Native peoples who spoke Carolina Iroquoian languages, especially the Tuscarora, as well as neighboring Indigenous communities in eastern North Carolina. The name Coharie is associated with “Schohari,” which has been interpreted as meaning “driftwood” in the Tuscarora language. Today, Coharie people primarily speak English, but their tribal identity remains connected to the history, place names and cultural memory of the Native communities who lived along the Coharie rivers.
Coharie.culture:
The Coharie have long lived in close relationship with the land, rivers and pine forests of southeastern North Carolina. Their communities developed around the Big and Little Coharie rivers, which supported fishing, farming, transportation and gathering. A Coharie legend says that when mothers heard strangers approaching their village, they gathered their children and softly whispered “Shhh” to keep them quiet until the strangers passed. Tribal tradition connects that sound to the wind moving through the whispering pines that still grow in Coharie homelands. 

Churches are central to Coharie community life. In the settlements of Holly Grove, New Bethel, Shiloh and Antioch, Indian churches serve as places of worship, family gathering, leadership, elder respect and social connection. Through these churches, families maintain relationships, pass down values and reinforce community responsibilities. Education has also been a major part of Coharie culture. By building their own schools and advocating for Native education, Coharie families protected their children’s identity during periods when state systems did not fully serve Native communities. 

Today, Coharie culture continues through tribal government, churches, schools, powwows, family networks, cultural arts and community service. The Coharie Tribe’s annual gatherings and programs help preserve history while supporting the needs of enrolled members. Their continued presence in Sampson and Harnett counties reflects a long history of resilience, adaptation and commitment to maintaining Coharie identity.

Congaree.description: The Congaree were a historic group of Native Americans who once lived within what is now central South Carolina, along the Congaree River. They spoke a language distinct from and not mutually intelligible with other local Siouan languages. The tribe joined the Catawba Nation in company of the Wateree several years after temporarily migrating to the Waccamaw River in 1732. During the middle of the eighteenth century, Congaree was considered one of the languages spoken within the Catawba Nation. The Congaree lived along the Santee and Congaree rivers, above and below the confluence of the Wateree River, in central South Carolina. According to James Mooney's 1894 history of the Siouan tribes, the Congaree occupied territory between the Santee tribe downriver of them and the Wateree tribe above. In Native American practice, people taken as captives in warfare, particularly women and children, were often kept or sold as slaves.
Congaree.language: They spoke a language distinct from and not mutually intelligible with other local Siouan languages. The language today is generally considered unclassified, though, some linguists believe that the language was related to Catawba.
Congaree.culture: Their town consisted of not more than 12 houses, with plantations up and down the country. Several tribes were generally small and lived closely adjoining one another, they differed in features, disposition, and language, a fact which renders the assignment of these small tribes to the Siouan family conjectural. They were a friendly people, handsome and well built, the women being especially beautiful compared with those of other tribes. In 1698, the Congaree lost most tribe members to smallpox. The Native Americans suffered high mortality from new infectious diseases that had become endemic for centuries among Europeans, leading to some acquired immunity for the latter.

Coree.description: The Coree (also Connamox, Cores, Corennines, Connamocksocks, Coranine Indians, Neuse River Indians) were a very small Native American tribe, who once occupied a coastal area south of the Neuse River in southeastern North Carolina in the area now covered by Carteret and Craven counties. Early 20th-century scholars were unsure of what language they spoke, but the coastal areas were mostly populated by Iroquois and Algonquian peoples. The Coree were not described by English colonists until 1701, by which time their population had already been reduced to as few as 125 members, likely due to epidemics of infectious disease and warfare. In the early 18th century, the Coree and several other tribes were allied with the Iroquoian Tuscarora against the colonists.
Coree.language: Their language may have been an Algonquian language like Powhatan.
Coree.culture: Coree Indians, when first encountered by Europeans arriving in what is now North Carolina, were living south of the Neuse River along the Atlantic Coast. Like other Indians of the Coastal Plain, the Coree (or Core) lived in villages, reportedly three in number, and depended for their livelihood on both agriculture and the ocean. The Coree indians mainly lived in one area and were hunter/gatherers and lived close to many streams, so fish were one of their main food sources. The Coree indians made wicker pottery and arrowheads, which can still be found today as well.

Croatan.description: The Croatan were a small Native American ethnic group living in the coastal areas of what is now North Carolina. They might have been a branch of the larger Roanoke people or allied with them. The Croatan lived in current Dare County, an area encompassing the Alligator River, Croatan Sound, Roanoke Island, Ocracoke Island, and parts of the Outer Banks, including Hatteras Island. The Croatan people who exist today live in Cumberland, Sampson, and Harnett counties predominantly. The chiefs, called werowances ("he who is rich"), controlled from 1 to 18 towns. The greatest were able to muster 700 or 800 fighting men. Chiefs and their families were held in great status and with respect, but they were not all-powerful.
Croatan.language: Carolina Algonquian (also known as Pamlico, Croatoan) was an Algonquian language of the Eastern Algonquian subgroup formerly spoken in North Carolina, United States. Carolina
Croatan.culture: Algonquian tribes had a varied diet that included fishing for salmon, sturgeon, and cod. They also hunted seals, walruses, whales, beavers, deer, geese, ducks, and pigeons. The Algonquian culture includes the different manifestations of social organization, types of housing, religion, and habits that the people had before the arrival of the colonizers. The social organization was based on the community. The houses changed according to the season of the year and could be shared with several families. The religion they practiced was animistic and they conducted trade using a primitive form of currency called, "wampun."

Cuttatawomen.description: The Cuttatawomen were a small Indigenous group associated with the lower Potomac River region of present-day Virginia and Maryland. They appear in colonial-era records as one of several Native communities connected to the broader Chesapeake world. Over time, the Cuttatawomen likely merged with other nearby groups as war, disease and English expansion disrupted Native communities.
Cuttatawomen.language: The Cuttatawomen likely spoke an Eastern Algonquian language related to other Chesapeake-area Native languages.
Cuttatawomen.culture: The Cuttatawomen lived in a river-based landscape where fishing, farming, hunting and trade were important. Their location near the Potomac connected them to neighboring towns and regional exchange networks. Like many smaller Chesapeake groups, their identity became harder to trace after the seventeenth century.

Doeg.description: The Doeg, also written as Tauxenent, Taux or Douge, were indigenous to northern Virginia. They lived along the Potomac River in what is now Fairfax and Prince William counties, including areas near Mason Neck, Occoquan and Quantico Creek. John Smith visited the Doeg village of Tauxenent in 1608, describing a settled community connected to the waterways of the Chesapeake region. As English settlement expanded in the mid-1600s, the Doeg were pushed from much of their homeland and many relocated toward Piscataway territory in Maryland or south toward the Rappahannock River, where they were eventually absorbed into other Native communities.
Doeg.language: The Doeg spoke an Algonquian language related to other Native languages of the Chesapeake region. Their language was not fully recorded, and much of what is known about them comes from colonial descriptions, place names and archaeology.
Doeg.culture: The Doeg lived in river-based communities where the Potomac, Occoquan and nearby creeks supported fishing, travel, trade and seasonal movement. Their larger villages included longhouses, palisades and leaders’ houses, while smaller camps were used for hunting, fishing, gathering plants and growing corn. Like other Chesapeake Algonquian peoples, they farmed corn, beans and squash, gathered nuts and berries, hunted deer and other animals, and harvested fish and shellfish. Waterways were central to Doeg life, connecting their villages to trade networks while also later making their lands more vulnerable to colonial expansion.

Eno.description: The Eno or Enoke, also called Stuckenock, was an American Indian tribe located in North Carolina during the 17th and 18th centuries that was later absorbed into the Catawba tribe in South Carolina along with various other smaller tribal bands. The Eno Indians were likely one of the loosely related tribes of Siouan-speaking Native Americans at the time of European exploration. By the early 18th century, the Enos, combined with the Shakoris, Tutelos, Saponis, Keyauwees, and Occaneechis, were reduced to a population of approximately 750 people. About 1715, the Enos merged with the Catawbas in the North Carolina-South Carolina border area; the Enos became "member elements of the Catawba" perhaps due to the outcome of the Yamassee War, in which the Enos may or may not have participated. The Eno Indians were a small tribe of North Carolina, allies of the Catawba Indians.
Eno.language: Their language was never recorded, but may have been a Siouan language similar to Catawba.
Eno.culture: The Eno village as surrounded by large cultivated fields and as built around a central plaza where the men played a game and was probably the chunkey game played with round stones among the Creeks. They were mound builders unlike any other Indians in North Carolina. On the Eno or on the lands near it, five historic tribes or subgroups appear to have lived. The Eno, Shocco and Adshusheer, all Sioux Indians with some mixture of ol-Algonquin, settled in villages and farmed.

Grigra.description: They were preceded in this area by what archaeologists call the indigenous Plaquemine culture, part of the larger, prehistoric Mississippian culture, which extended throughout the lower Mississippi Valley and its tributaries. Its largest center was at Cahokia in present-day Illinois near the confluence of the Illinois, Missouri and Mississippi rivers. Its peoples are noted for their hierarchical communities, building complex earthworks and platform mound architecture, and intensively cultivating maize. Archaeological evidence indicates that people of the Plaquemine culture, an elaboration of the Coles Creek culture, had lived in the Natchez Bluffs region since at least as long ago as 700 CE.
Grigra.language: Their language was never recorded, but was possibly related to Natchez
Grigra.culture: Archaeologists believe that the Grigra adopted the Natchez culture. This includes Sun worshiping, stickball, mound building, and membership was tracked by the female line. The name Grigra was given to this tribe by the French, based on their frequent use of the term “grigra” in their speech. Like many other Indigenous groups in the southeast, their society was based on female kinship. Women controlled family farms, and men married into female lineages. The principal crops were corn, beans, squash, wild and semi-domesticated grains, tubers, fruits, and nuts. Armed with bows and arrows and guns acquired through trade with Europeans, hunters killed deer, bear, and smaller game. Fish were also abundant in the Mississippi River and the numerous lakes and streams throughout the region.

Guacata.description: The Guacata are first mentioned by Fontaneda (1854), who in one place speaks of them as on Lake Mayaimi (Okeechobee), but this probably means only that they ranged across to the lake from the eastern seacoast. Shortly after his conquest of Florida Menendez left 200 men in the Ais country, but the Indians of that tribe soon rose against them and they moved to the neighborhood of the Guacata, where they were so well treated that they called the place Santa Lucia. Next year, however, these Indians rose against them and although they were at first defeated the Spaniards were so hard pressed that they abandoned the place in 1568. They were still an independent body in the time of Dickenson, in 1699, but not long afterward they evidently united with other east coast bands, and they were probably part of those who emigrated to Cuba in 1763.
Guacata.language: Guacata and Tequesta people is related to the Tunica language.
Guacata.culture: The economy of the Southeast was mostly agricultural. The leading crop was corn, followed by beans and squash. Southeast peoples grew several varieties of corn. Some varieties were baked or roasted on the cob, and some were boiled into succotash—a dish of stewed corn and beans. Other varieties were pounded into hominy or cornmeal. Some corn, beans, and squash were dried and stored for later use. Southeast peoples also raised sunflowers, which were processed for their oil, and tobacco. Wild plant foods, including greens, berries, nuts, acorns, and sap, were acquired through gathering. Wild game was abundant in most of the Southeast. Native peoples hunted deer, elk, black bears, beavers, squirrels, rabbits, otters, raccoons, and turkeys. In what is now the state of Florida, the diet included turtles and alligators.

Guale.description: Guale was a historic Native American chiefdom of Mississippian culture peoples located along the coast of present-day Georgia and the Sea Islands. Spanish Florida established its Roman Catholic missionary system in the chiefdom in the late 16th century. During the late 17th century and early 18th century, Guale society was shattered by extensive epidemics of new infectious diseases and attacks by other tribes. Some of the surviving remnants migrated to the mission areas of Spanish Florida, while others remained near the Georgia coast. Joining with other survivors, they became known as the Yamasee, an ethnically mixed group that emerged in a process of ethnogenesis. The extent of population loss in the province due to disease and fugitivism was concealed by an influx of Yamasees, whom the Guales sent to do their labor service.
Guale.language: Scholars have not reached a consensus on how to classify the Guale language. Early claims that the Guale spoke a Muskogean language
Guale.culture: The prehistoric people were organized into chiefdoms. They built Mississippian-type platform mounds, major earthworks requiring the organized labor of many people, and using highly skilled soil and engineering knowledge. They used the mounds for ceremonial, religious and burial purposes. French explorers under Jean Ribault contacted the Guale, whom they called the Oade after their chief, during their voyage to the Atlantic coast of North America in 1562. The Guale maintained good relations with the ephemeral French settlement known as Charlesfort on Parris Island in what is now South Carolina.

Hatteras.description: The Hatteras were a small Indigenous group who lived on the sound side of Hatteras Island near Cape Hatteras, North Carolina. Their village was often referred to in early records as Indian Town or Cape Hatteras Indian Town. Some writers have connected them to the earlier Croatoan people of the Roanoke era, but that connection is not certain. By the early 1700s, the Hatteras population was very small, and by the mid-1700s many had moved to the mainland and blended with nearby Native communities, including the Machapunga, Roanoke and Mattamuskeet.
Hatteras.language: The Hatteras likely spoke an Algonquian language related to other coastal Carolina Native languages. Their specific language was not recorded in detail.
Hatteras.culture: The Hatteras lived in a coastal environment shaped by the Outer Banks, sounds, marshes and ocean waters. They relied heavily on fishing, shellfish gathering, hunting and seasonal coastal resources. Early accounts mention Hatteras people catching fish along the sea sands, showing their close relationship with the shoreline. Their location near Cape Hatteras connected them to other Native communities of the Carolina sounds, as well as early English colonial trade and contact. By the late 1700s, the Hatteras no longer appeared as a separate tribal community in written records.

Hitchiti.description: The Hitchiti were a Muskogean-speaking tribe formerly residing chiefly in a town of the same name on the east bank of the Chattahoochee River, 4 miles below Chiaha, in west Georgia. They spoke the Hitchiti language, which was mutually intelligible with Mikasuki; both tribes were part of the loose Creek confederacy. The latter language is still spoken by the Miccsukee tribe of Florida, as well as many Seminole. The ancestors of at least some of the people in the area may have been there as early as 12,000 years ago.
