A variant of the Lamar regional culture, with influences from the Fort Walton culture to the south, developed in the towns along the Chattahoochee between 1300 and 1400.
Hitchiti.language: They spoke the Hitchiti language, which was mutually intelligible with Mikasuki; both tribes were part of the loose Creek confederacy.
Hitchiti.culture: The Hitchiti were absorbed into and became an integral part of the Creek Nation, though preserving to a large extent their own language and customs. Similarly, those Mikasuki-speakers who joined the Lower Creek migrations to Florida maintained their culture. For years after being included among the Seminole, which formed from remnant peoples in Florida, in 1962 they gained federal recognition as the Miccsukee Tribe of Indians in Florida. Each town had a plaza or community square, around which were grouped the houses—rectangular structures with four vertical walls of poles plastered over with mud to form wattle. The roofs were pitched and covered with either bark or thatch, with smoke holes left open at the gables. The plaza was the gathering point for such important religious observances as the Busk, or Green Corn, ceremony, an annual first-fruits and new-fire rite.

Hopewell Culture.description: The Hopewell culture refers to ancient Indigenous communities that flourished from about 200 BCE to 500 CE, centered mainly in what is now southern Ohio. Related Hopewell sites have also been found across parts of the Midwest and eastern North America, including Indiana, Illinois, Michigan, Wisconsin, Iowa, Kansas, Pennsylvania and New York. The name comes from the Hopewell farm in Ross County, Ohio, where major earthworks and burial mounds were excavated. Hopewell communities are best known for their large earthworks, burial mounds, long-distance trade networks and finely made ceremonial objects.
Hopewell Culture.language: The language or languages spoken by Hopewell communities were not recorded. Because Hopewell culture existed long before written records in the region, knowledge of their language comes only through archaeological interpretation, not direct documentation.
Hopewell Culture.culture: Hopewell people lived in small villages often located near rivers and streams. They farmed crops such as corn, squash and sunflowers, while also hunting, fishing and gathering nuts, fruits, seeds and roots. They built many kinds of earthworks, including burial mounds and geometric enclosures, which likely served ceremonial, social and spiritual purposes. Hopewell artisans created pottery, copper ornaments, mica cutouts, stone tools and polished effigy pipes shaped like animals. Materials such as copper, mica, obsidian, seashells and pearls show that Hopewell communities were connected to trade networks stretching from the Rocky Mountains to the Gulf and Atlantic coasts.

Houma.description: The Houma are a historic Native American people of Louisiana on the east side of the Red River of the South. Their descendants, the Houma people or the United Houma Nation, have been recognized by the state as a tribe since 1972, but are not recognized by the federal government. According to the tribe, as of 2023 they have more than 17,000 enrolled tribal citizens residing within a six-parish area that encompasses 4,750 square miles. The parishes are St. Mary, Terrebonne, Lafourche, Jefferson, Plaquemines, and St. Bernard. The Houma tribe, was recorded by the French explorer René-Robert Cavelier, Sieur de La Salle, in 1682 as living along the Red River on the east side of Mississippi River. Because their war emblem is the saktce-ho'ma, or Red Crawfish, the anthropologist John R. Swanton speculated that the Houma are an offshoot of the Yazoo River region's Chakchiuma tribe, whose name derives from saktce-ho'ma.
Houma.language: Houma (Houma: uma) is a Western Muskogean language that was spoken in the Central and Lower Mississippi Valley by the indigenous Houma people.
Houma.culture: The Houma continue to have a hunter-gatherer type economy, which he documented, depending on the bayous and swamps for fish and game. They also cultivate small subsistence gardens. By the time of European contact, most of these Native American tribes had settled in villages of 500 people or fewer, and grew corn, beans, squash, sunflowers, greens, tobacco, and other crops. The southeast Native Americans also gathered berries, nuts, wild plants, and roots from the surrounding forests. For the most part, women tended the fields while men hunted, fished, and engaged in trade with one another, as well as with other groups to the north and west.

Jeaga (Jaega).description: At the time of the Spanish discovery of Florida, the Jeaga (“hay-gwa”) lived along Florida’s east coast in the modern St. Lucie County area. They utilized the rich coastal resources as well as the flatlands inland from the coast for 10 -20 miles, possibly further. The Jaega (also Jega, Xega, Geiga) were Native Americans living in a chiefdom of the same name, which included the coastal parts of present-day Martin County and northern Palm Beach County, Florida at the time of initial European contact, and until the 18th century. The name Jobe, or Jove, has been identified as a synonym of Jaega, a sub-group of the Jaega, or a town of the Jaega. Throughout the 16th and 17th centuries, the Jeaga, particularly those in Jobe, gained power and influence by salvaging materials from ships wrecked along the coast of Palm Beach County.
Jeaga (Jaega).language: The Ais and Jaega languages have been tentatively assigned by some authors to the Muskogean language family, and by others to the Arawakan language family.
Jeaga (Jaega).culture: The Jeagas foraged for coco plums, sea grapes and palm berries, They also drank a frothy, ceremonial tea made from the roasted leaves of the cassina plant. Known as the "black drink", it was high in caffeine and obviously made quite an impression on the Europeans. The Jeaga were masters at carving dugout canoes from cypress trees and expertly navigated Florida’s waterways to Lake Okeechobee and throughout South Florida to trade with other tribes. Sharks, manatees, deer, squirrel, sea turtles, mullet, snapper, sea grapes, coco plums, and saw palmetto berries were some of nature’s resources that fed the early people. They lived in wigwam-style homes consisting of wooden poles stuck into the ground and covered with palmetto leaves.

Kaskaskia.description: The Kaskaskia were one of the principal tribes of the Illinois Confederacy, also known as the Illini. Their early villages were located along the Illinois River, including a major settlement near present-day Utica, Illinois, close to Starved Rock. French missionaries visited the Kaskaskia in the 1670s, and the village became an important center of Native and French interaction. Around 1700, the Kaskaskia moved south near the mouth of the Kaskaskia River, where they remained for many years. Warfare, disease, alcohol, land loss and U.S. treaty pressure greatly reduced their population. In the 1800s, the Kaskaskia joined with the Peoria, Wea and Piankashaw, and their descendants are now part of the Peoria Tribe of Indians of Oklahoma.
Kaskaskia.language: The Kaskaskia spoke a language in the Algonquian language family. Their language was closely related to the languages of other Illinois Confederacy peoples, including the Peoria, Cahokia, Tamaroa and Michigamea.
Kaskaskia.culture: The Kaskaskia lived in large villages and were closely connected to river systems that supported farming, hunting, fishing and trade. They grew crops such as corn, beans and squash, while also hunting game and gathering seasonal foods. Their communities became important places of exchange between Native peoples and French traders and missionaries. Over time, the Kaskaskia joined with other Illinois peoples for survival as disease, warfare and colonial expansion disrupted their homelands. Their history continues through the Peoria Tribe of Indians of Oklahoma.

Kauwets’a:ka.description: The Kauwets’a:ka, commonly known as the Meherrin, are an Indigenous nation with ancestral homelands in southeastern Virginia and northeastern North Carolina. Their traditional name means “People of the Water,” reflecting their connection to the rivers and waterways of the region. The first known written account of the Meherrin was in 1650, when an English expedition visited the village of Cowonchahawkon, near present-day Emporia, Virginia. Over time, colonial pressure pushed the Meherrin south into North Carolina, where they established communities in areas that are now Hertford, Bertie, Northampton and Gates counties. The Meherrin received state recognition from North Carolina in 1986 and continue to maintain tribal government, cultural programs and an annual powwow.
Kauwets’a:ka.language: The Meherrin language is known as Skarù·ręʔ and is closely related to Tuscarora. The Meherrin are part of the wider Haudenosaunee cultural and linguistic world, with ties to Tuscarora, Nottoway, Cherokee and other related nations.
Kauwets’a:ka.culture: Meherrin culture is rooted in water, land, community and continuity. Their communities developed near rivers and creeks that supported farming, fishing, travel and trade. The Meherrin maintained political leadership through chiefs and councils, and today the tribe is led by a chief and elected council members. Cultural life continues through language revitalization, spirituality, traditional teachings, environmental stewardship and the annual Meherrin Indian Powwow held near Ahoskie and Murfreesboro, North Carolina. The tribe emphasizes preserving its heritage for future generations while continuing to serve its community today.

Kecoughtan.description: The Kecoughtan were an Indigenous people whose town stood near the mouth of the Hampton River in present-day Hampton, Virginia. When English settlers arrived in the early 1600s, Kecoughtan was an ancient settlement surrounded by cleared fields and located near important waterways. Powhatan had recently taken control of the town and repopulated it with people loyal to his authority. Relations with the English soon worsened, and in 1610 Governor Thomas Gates forced the Native residents from Kecoughtan. The English later renamed the area Elizabeth City, but the name Kecoughtan has survived in local history.
Kecoughtan.language: The Kecoughtan spoke a language connected to the Native peoples of the Powhatan world in tidewater Virginia. Their specific language was not preserved in detail, but it was part of the broader Indigenous language landscape of the Chesapeake region.
Kecoughtan.culture: Kecoughtan was located in a rich coastal environment with access to rivers, oysters, fish, birds, farmland and trade routes. Early English accounts describe the Kecoughtan people as providing food and shelter to starving Jamestown colonists, including oysters, fish, fowl, bread and warm houses. Their community likely relied on farming, fishing, gathering and hunting, supported by the cleared fields and waterways around Hampton Roads. Although the Native town was displaced by English settlement, the name Kecoughtan remains tied to the Indigenous history of Hampton, Virginia.

Keyauwee.description: The Keyauwee Indians were a small North Carolina tribe, native to the area of present day Randolph County, North Carolina. The Keyauwee village was surrounded by palisades and cornfields about thirty miles northeast of the Yadkin River, near present day High Point, North Carolina. The Keyauwee village was vulnerable to attack, so the Keyauwee constantly joined with other tribes for better protection. They joined with the Tutelo, Saponi, Occaneechi, and the Shakori tribes, moving to the Albemarle Sound with the last two for a settlement that would later be foiled. The Keyauwee would move further southward along with the Cheraw and Peedee tribes, close along the border of the two Carolinas, where they conducted deerskin trade with Charleston traders and allied with the Indian neighbors in the Yamassee War. Eventually, their tribe name vanished from historical records, and with time, they were absorbed by the Catawba tribe.
Keyauwee.language: The Keyauwee tribe merged with local tribes throughout their time due to factors like geographic proximity and Siouan language.
Keyauwee.culture: In 1701, English explorer John Lawson noted of finding them in a palisaded village about 30 miles north east of Yadkin River, near the present Highpoint, Guilford County, North Carolina. Around the village were large fields of corn. At that time they were about equal in number to the Saponi and had, as chief, Keyauwee Jack, who was by birth a Congaree, but had obtained the chieftaincy by marriage with their “queen.” Lawson stated the men wore mustaches or whiskers, an unusual custom for Indians.

Kiikaapoi (Kickapoo).description: The Kiikaapoi (Kickapoo) are an Indigenous people whose ancestral homelands were in the Great Lakes region, particularly present-day Michigan. Over time, conflicts with other tribes and increasing pressure from European colonization pushed them westward into Wisconsin, Illinois, Missouri, Kansas, Oklahoma, Texas, and northern Mexico. Known for their independence and resistance to outside control, the Kickapoo worked to preserve their traditions despite repeated displacement. Today, Kickapoo communities continue to live in Kansas, Oklahoma, Texas, and Mexico.
Kiikaapoi (Kickapoo).language: The Kickapoo language belongs to the Algonquian language family and is closely related to the languages of the Sauk and Fox peoples. Efforts to preserve and teach the language continue within modern Kickapoo communities.
Kiikaapoi (Kickapoo).culture: The Kickapoo traditionally lived in permanent villages during the growing season, cultivating corn, beans, and squash, while hunting and gathering supplemented their diet. During the fall and winter, many families traveled to hunt game, including buffalo. They were known for woodworking, pottery, and the creation of cradleboards, bowls, and other tools. Spiritual life centered on a Creator and a rich tradition of stories, ceremonies, and sacred bundles. Despite centuries of relocation and outside pressures, the Kickapoo have maintained a strong cultural identity and are widely recognized for preserving many of their traditional customs and beliefs.

Kiskiack.description: The Kiskiack, also spelled Chiskiack, were an Indigenous people whose homeland was along the south bank of the York River in present-day York County, Virginia. Their name has been interpreted as meaning “wide land” or “broad place.” The Kiskiack lived in permanent villages on the Virginia Peninsula and were one of the early groups connected to Powhatan’s political network. Their leader, or werowance, was Ottahotin, who guided John Smith during a 1608 journey to meet Powhatan at Werowocomoco. English expansion and warfare eventually forced the Kiskiack from their York River homeland; by the mid-1600s, they had relocated near the Piankatank River, where they were granted reservation land.
Kiskiack.language: The Kiskiack spoke a language connected to the Native peoples of the Powhatan world in tidewater Virginia. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Kiskiack.culture: The Kiskiack lived in settled villages made up of longhouses, also called yihakans, where related families shared private and communal space. Their York River homeland supported farming, fishing, hunting, gathering and trade. Before English settlement expanded, the Kiskiack occupied a strategic location near waterways that connected them to other Native towns across the Chesapeake region. Their history is closely tied to the early colonial transformation of the Virginia Peninsula, including conflict, displacement and the survival of their name in local history.

Koasati (Coushatta).description: The Coushatta (Koasati: Koasati, Kowassaati or Kowassati) are now living primarily in the U.S. states of Louisiana, Oklahoma, and Texas. When first encountered by Europeans, they lived in the territory of present-day Tennessee, Georgia and Alabama. They were historically closely allied and intermarried with the Alabama people, also members of the Creek Confederacy. Their languages are closely related and mutually intelligible. Under pressure from Anglo-American colonial settlement after 1763 and the French defeat in the Seven Years' War, they began to move west into Mississippi, Louisiana and Texas, which were then under Spanish rule.
Koasati (Coushatta).language: Koasati (also Coushatta) is a Native American language of Muskogean origin.
Koasati (Coushatta).culture: The Coushatta were traditionally agriculturalists, growing a variety of maize, beans, and squash, and supplementing their diet by hunting game and fish. They are known for their skill at basketry. Nearly all the Spanish expeditions (including the 1539-1543 Hernando de Soto Expedition) into the interior of Spanish Florida recorded encountering the original town of the tribe. Music holds a special place in Koasati culture, with traditional songs and dances playing a significant role in their ceremonies and celebrations. Storytelling is an integral part of Koasati tradition, serving as a means of preserving their history, teachings, and cultural values.

Koroa.description: The Koroa are one of many “small tribes” of the southeastern United States that are mentioned briefly in historic accounts and then fade from the records during the colonial period. The Koroa were one of the groups of indigenous people who lived in the Mississippi Valley prior to the European settlement of the region. They lived in the northwest of present-day Mississippi in the Yazoo River basin. The Koroa may be the tribe identified by Hernando de Soto's expedition as the Coligua or Cologoa. They were met by Soto's company in the area of what is today Little Rock, Arkansas, in 1541.
Koroa.language: Their language was never well recorded, but appears to have been a Gulf language, either a dialect of Tunica or a closely related language.
Koroa.culture: Tunica Indians grew crops of corn and squash that were the primary food staples. The men did most of the gardening and the women collected wild plant foods, including fruits, berries, nuts, seeds, roots, and herbs. The men also hunted deer, bear, and occasionally buffalo for meat, hides, and other products. Water from salty springs was evaporated to make salt, some of which was traded to other tribes. The men wore deerskin loincloths during the warm seasons, and they tattooed themselves and wore beads and pendants. Women wore short, fringed skirts of cloth made by pounding the inner bark of mulberry trees. They also tattooed themselves and wore beads, pendants and ear ornaments. Women kept their hair in a single, long braid that often was coiled on top of the head. Both sexes wore cloaks of mulberry cloth, woven turkey feathers, or muskrat fur during the cold seasons.

Kusso.description: The Kusso are an Indigenous people of the South Carolina Lowcountry with documented history from at least the late 1600s. Around 1700, Kusso ancestors were granted land near the Edisto River, also known historically as Pon-Pon. The community also includes descendants of Natchez people who fled conflict with the French in the 1700s and later settled near the Edisto River under English protection. In 1970, the tribe officially adopted the name Edisto because of its long connection to the Edisto River and surrounding communities. Today, many tribal members remain connected to communities such as Creeltown and Four Holes, with families also living in Dorchester, Colleton, Berkeley and Charleston counties.
Kusso.language: The Kusso language was not fully preserved in written records. The tribe’s ancestry includes Kusso and Natchez roots, and Natchez was a distinct Indigenous language of the lower Mississippi Valley.
Kusso.culture: Kusso and Edisto identity is strongly tied to land, family, community and the Edisto River region. Tribal families maintained community life through rural settlements, schools, churches and local leadership, even as members moved off the original reservation lands during the 1800s. In the twentieth century, the community organized to protect education and civil rights, especially after the closing of the last Indian school in South Carolina. The Four Hole Indian Freedom Organization, founded in 1969, helped the tribe regroup, elect leadership and continue efforts to preserve Edisto Natchez-Kusso identity and history

Lumbee.description: The Lumbee are a Native American people primarily centered in Robeson, Hoke, Cumberland, and Scotland counties in North Carolina. As of 2025, the Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina is a federally-recognized tribe in North Carolina numbering approximately 55,000 enrolled members. The Lumbee take their name from the Lumber River, which winds through Robeson County. Pembroke, North Carolina, is their economic, cultural, and political center. According to the 2000 United States census report, 89% of the population of the town of Pembroke identified as Lumbee; 40% of Robeson County's population identified as Lumbee. Archaeological evidence reveals that the area now known as Robeson County (central to modern Lumbee territory) has been continuously occupied by Native people for at least 14,000 years.
Lumbee.language: Lumbee people speak both mainstream varieties of English and a vernacular form, Lumbee English.
Lumbee.culture: Southeast peoples believed that animal spirits were capable of carrying out revenge against humans. Because of this, hunting was a sacred act that involved ritual, sacrifice, and strict regulations. They also believed that plants had spiritual power, but plants were considered friendly to humans. Many cultural traditions reported by the earliest European explorers, such as the use of ceremonial mounds, the heavy reliance on corn (maize), and the importance of social stratification in some areas, were clearly developed. Women were responsible for cultivating the fields, gathering wild plant foods, cooking and preserving food, taking care of young children and elders, and manufacturing cordage, baskets, pottery, clothing, and other goods. Men assumed duties associated with war, trade, and the hunt.

Manahoac.description: The Manahoac were an Indigenous people of the Virginia Piedmont and Appalachian foothills, especially in areas near the upper Rappahannock, Rapidan, Potomac and North Anna rivers. Early English observers described them as living in several villages and maintaining close ties with the Monacan. Their homeland served as an important hunting ground and travel corridor between the mountains, Piedmont and coastal regions. Disease, warfare, English expansion and pressure from neighboring groups displaced the Manahoac during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. By the late colonial period, they were no longer recorded as a distinct tribal community.
Manahoac.language: The Manahoac language is generally understood to have belonged to the Siouan language family and was related to Monacan and other Piedmont Native languages. Some records are inconsistent, but most historical interpretations connect the Manahoac to the Siouan-speaking peoples of interior Virginia.
Manahoac.culture: The Manahoac lived in the woodlands and foothills of northern and central Virginia, where they hunted, fished, gathered and farmed. They grew crops such as corn and squash, but also relied heavily on deer, buffalo and other game. Some Manahoac communities used controlled burning to open forested areas, create grasslands and attract animals for hunting. Their villages and travel routes connected them to neighboring Native nations, including the Monacan, while also placing them in conflict with groups such as the Iroquois and Powhatan.

Mascogo.description: The Mascogo, often known as Black Seminoles, are descendants of Africans who escaped slavery and built alliances with Seminole communities in Florida. Many lived in their own villages, farmed independently and fought alongside Seminoles against slave hunters, colonial expansion and the U.S. Army. After forced removal to Indian Territory and later relocation pressures in Texas, a group of Mascogos crossed into Mexico in 1850, where slavery had already been abolished. They founded Nacimiento in Coahuila, Mexico, which remains an important Mascogo homeland today. Their history is shaped by freedom-seeking, migration, military service, borderlands survival and cultural resilience.
Mascogo.language: Mascogo people have historically spoken Afro-Seminole Creole, along with Spanish, English and Seminole-associated languages depending on community location. Language use reflects their movement across Florida, Oklahoma, Texas and Mexico.
Mascogo.culture: Mascogo culture blends African, Seminole, Mexican and borderlands traditions. Their communities have long relied on farming, livestock, military service, family networks and cultural memory to survive displacement and discrimination. In Nacimiento, Mascogo families continue to honor Juneteenth as a celebration of freedom, even though their ancestors had already freed themselves before emancipation reached Texas. Traditional foods, family stories and community ties remain central to Mascogo identity, especially as many descendants now move between Mexico and the United States for work while maintaining ties to Nacimiento.

Massawomeck.description: The Massawomeck were an Indigenous people known to early English colonists in the Chesapeake region. They lived northwest of the Chesapeake Bay and were associated with the upper Potomac, Ohio Valley and Allegheny Plateau region. Early accounts describe them as a populous nation whose warriors traveled long distances to raid and trade with communities near the Potomac and Chesapeake. By the mid-1600s, warfare, disease, shifting trade routes and pressure from the Haudenosaunee and Susquehannock disrupted Massawomeck communities. Many survivors likely merged with other Native peoples, including groups later known as Mingo, Susquehannock, Westo or other Iroquoian-descended communities.
Massawomeck.language: The Massawomeck language was not fully recorded. They are generally believed to have spoken an Iroquoian language related to the languages of nearby interior groups such as the Susquehannock and Erie.
Massawomeck.culture: The Massawomeck were known for long-distance travel, trade, warfare and skilled canoe use. John Smith described their bark canoes, shields, baskets, pipes, bows, arrows and other goods as different from those of the Chesapeake peoples he knew. They traded furs, especially beaver skins, and obtained European goods through northern trade routes connected to the St. Lawrence River and later through English traders on the Potomac. Their towns were reportedly protected by palisades, and their regional power came from their mobility, trade connections and military strength.

Matecumbe.description: The Matecumbe were an Indigenous people of the Florida Keys, especially connected to Upper and Lower Matecumbe Keys and nearby islands such as Tea Table, Indian and Lignumvitae Keys. The name Matecumbe appears in Spanish records as early as the sixteenth century and is one of the oldest South Florida place names still used for roughly the same area. The Matecumbe were likely a smaller group whose political relationships shifted between more powerful South Florida chiefdoms, including the Calusa and Tequesta. Spanish records mention a leader called the Cacique Matecumbe in the 1500s, showing the importance of the area during early colonial contact. By the 1700s, Native communities in the Keys had greatly declined because of disease, slave raids, colonial violence, missionization and movement to Cuba.
Matecumbe.language: The Matecumbe language was not directly recorded. It was likely related to the languages of southern Florida groups such as the Calusa or Tequesta, though its exact classification remains uncertain.
Matecumbe.culture: The Matecumbe lived in an island and marine environment shaped by reefs, channels, fish, shellfish, turtles and canoe travel. Their diet included fish, turtle, mollusks, lobster and other coastal foods, and they moved between islands according to available resources. Early accounts describe Keys peoples as skilled archers, canoe travelers and divers, with strong knowledge of the waters around them. They also traded with Europeans and sometimes assisted shipwrecked travelers, while shipwrecks brought both goods and danger to the region. Their history reflects the importance of the Florida Keys as a crossroads between South Florida, Cuba and the wider Caribbean.

Mattamuskeet.description: The Mattamuskeet, also known as the Machapunga or Marimiskeet, were an Indigenous people of present-day Hyde County, North Carolina. Their homeland centered around Lake Mattamuskeet, a large and important lake surrounded by marshes, low ridges and coastal waterways. By the early 1700s, their population had declined to a single village, and they later joined Native allies in the Tuscarora War against European settlers. After the war, Mattamuskeet, Coree and other Native survivors were placed on a reservation near Lake Mattamuskeet, but settler pressure and land sales gradually dissolved the reservation by the late 1700s.
Mattamuskeet.language: The Mattamuskeet/Machapunga language was part of the Algonquian language family and was related to other coastal Carolina Native languages. Much of the language was not preserved in written records.
Mattamuskeet.culture: The Mattamuskeet were known as skilled watermen who lived from the lake, marshes, rivers and sounds of coastal North Carolina. They fished, crabbed, gathered shellfish, hunted and farmed crops such as corn, beans, squash, knotweed and sunflowers. Women played important roles in governance, healing and community defense. Even after colonial records reclassified many descendants as “free persons of color,” families with names such as Mackey, Squires, Long Tom, Collins, Barber, Chance, Clayton and Bryant preserved ties to Mattamuskeet ancestry, land and community memory.

Mattaponi.description: The Mattaponi are an Indigenous people of eastern Virginia whose homeland is along the Mattaponi River in present-day King William County. The Mattaponi have lived in the region for thousands of years and were part of the larger Powhatan political world of the Chesapeake Bay area. Their reservation was established in 1658 and remains a central part of Mattaponi identity, culture and sovereignty. The Mattaponi endured land loss, colonial warfare, forced assimilation and Virginia’s Racial Integrity Act, but they continued to preserve their community and traditions. Today, the tribe is governed by a chief, assistant chief and council members, and continues to protect its reservation land and river.
Mattaponi.language: The Mattaponi language is part of the Algonquian language family. Although the language was nearly lost through forced assimilation, the tribe continues efforts to reclaim and preserve this important part of its heritage.
Mattaponi.culture: Mattaponi culture is deeply connected to the Mattaponi River, which has long supported fishing, transportation and traditional practices. Tribal members continue to farm, garden, fish, hunt and trap, blending older traditions with modern community life. The riverbank provides clay used for pottery, and Mattaponi artisans continue pottery and gourd work inspired by ancestral methods as well as contemporary designs. The reservation includes important community places such as Mattaponi Indian Baptist Church, the Mattaponi Tribal Museum, a former reservation school building and a fish hatchery focused on American shad restoration.

Mayaimi.description: The Mayaimi were Native American people who lived around Lake Mayaimi (now Lake Okeechobee) in the Belle Glade area of Florida from the beginning of the Common Era until the 17th or 18th century. In the languages of the Mayaimi, Calusa, and Tequesta tribes, Mayaimi meant "big water." The current name, Okeechobee, is derived from the Hitchiti word meaning "big water". The Mayaimis have no linguistic or cultural relationship with the Miami people of the Great Lakes region. The city of Miami is named after the Miami River, which derived its name from Lake Mayaimi. The Mayaimis have no linguistic or cultural relationship with the Miami people of the Great Lakes region. The city of Miami is named after the Miami River, which derived its name from Lake Mayaimi.
Mayaimi.language: The language of the Calusa, Mayaimi and Tequesta people is related to the Tunica language.
Mayaimi.culture: Living on the Atlantic coast, the Mayaimi were a fishing community with fighting villages all along the coast. Unlike the northern tribes, the Mayaimi were coastal mound builders who built large mounded villages near modern day Lake Okeechobee. The Mayaimis built ceremonial and village earthwork mounds around Lake Okeechobee similar to those of the Mississippian culture and earlier mound builders. They dug many canals as other earthworks, to use as pathways for their canoes. The dugout canoes were a platform type with shovel-shaped ends, resembling those used in Central America and the West Indies, rather than the pointed-end canoes used by other peoples in the southeastern United States.

Miccosukee.description: The Miccosukee Tribe of Indians is a federally recognized Native American tribe in the U.S. state of Florida. Together with the Seminole Nation of Oklahoma and the Seminole Tribe of Florida, it is one of three federally recognized Seminole entities. he Miccosukee were displaced during the Seminole Wars (1817–1858), a series of three military conflicts between the United States and the Seminole people. Historically, the Miccosukees were a group that moved between present-day Georgia and northern Florida, with an extended range for hunting, fishing, and trading expeditions stretching from the Appalachian Mountains to the Florida Keys. By the late 1700s, Miccosukee-speaking villages had been built in the Everglades.
Miccosukee.language: The Miccosukee, along with the Florida Seminole, speak the Mikasuki language, also spelled Miccosukee.
Miccosukee.culture: The Miccosukee and other Lower Creek tribes lived together in harmony. They shared legends, religious practices and social gatherings, in addition to trade and traditional stickball games. They lived by hunting, fishing and growing crops and corn being the most significant. The new harvest is still celebrated each year at the sacred Green Corn Dance. The Miccosukee people lived in settled villages of houses called chickees. Chickees were made of wood and plaster, and the roofs were thatched with palmetto fiber. Miccosukee men wore loincloths. Miccosukee women wore wraparound skirts woven from palmetto fiber. Shirts were not necessary in Miccosukee culture, but the Miccosukees did wear mantles in cool weather.

Monacan.description: The Monacan Indian Nation is a federally recognized sovereign tribe headquartered on Bear Mountain in Amherst County, Virginia. Their ancestral territory includes Virginia west of the fall line, parts of southeastern West Virginia and portions of northern North Carolina. Monacan people have lived in the Virginia Piedmont for thousands of years, with communities once organized in villages across the region. In the 1800s, Monacan families established the Bear Mountain settlement, which remains central to tribal identity today. The Monacan Indian Nation received state recognition in 1989 and federal recognition in 2018.
Monacan.language: The Monacan language belonged to the Siouan language family. Although the original language was disrupted by colonization and assimilation, the tribe continues to preserve cultural knowledge connected to its heritage.
Monacan.culture: Monacan culture is rooted in the Piedmont landscape, Bear Mountain, river valleys, forests and ancestral village sites. Monacan people traditionally farmed crops such as corn, beans and squash, while also hunting deer and small game and gathering fruits, nuts and wild plants. The tribe has long maintained traditions of community gathering, pottery, religious life, education and cultural preservation. Despite racial reclassification under Virginia’s Racial Integrity Act and other pressures to assimilate, Monacan families preserved their identity and continue to celebrate their culture through tribal programs, a museum, homecoming events and an annual powwow.

Moneton.description: The Moneton were an Indigenous people associated with present-day West Virginia, especially the Kanawha Valley near the Kanawha and New rivers. Their name has been interpreted as meaning “Big Water” people, reflecting their connection to major river systems. In 1671, traders Thomas Batts and Robert Fallam visited the Moneton, and a few years later Gabriel Arthur described them as living in a large town. After the late 1600s, the Moneton largely disappeared from written records, likely because disease, warfare and pressure from neighboring groups caused survivors to merge with other Native communities in the Virginia Piedmont and surrounding regions. They might have been related to the Shawnee, an Algonquian-speaking people.
Moneton.language: The Moneton language belonged to the Siouan language family. Very little of the language was recorded, so much of what is known about the Moneton comes from early colonial accounts and regional history rather than preserved vocabulary.
Moneton.culture: The Moneton lived in a river valley environment shaped by forests, wetlands, mountains and waterways. They likely farmed crops such as corn, beans and squash while also hunting deer, fishing and gathering nuts, roots, berries and other seasonal foods. Some accounts suggest they lived in matrilineal clans and practiced important initiation ceremonies. Moneton identity was also marked by distinctive long hair and tattoos, which helped distinguish them from some neighboring peoples. Their history reflects the disruption many interior Native communities faced from disease, warfare, migration and colonial expansion.

Moratok.description: The Moratok, also spelled Moratoc, were an Indigenous people associated with the Roanoke River region of present-day North Carolina and Virginia. Their name may have originally referred to a place, though its meaning is uncertain. The Moratok are mentioned in accounts from the Raleigh expeditions, which described them as living far upriver from the Roanoke Island settlement. Very little is recorded about them, and they no longer exist as a distinct tribe. Survivors likely merged with neighboring Native communities after disease, warfare and colonial disruption.
Moratok.language: The Moratok language was not properly recorded. It was likely related to the languages of other Native communities in the Carolina sound and Roanoke River region.
Moratok.culture: The Moratok lived near the Roanoke River, a landscape that supported fishing, farming, hunting, gathering and travel. Their known village shared the same name as the tribe, but few details about the settlement were preserved. Like neighboring communities, they likely relied on corn agriculture along with seasonal foods from rivers, forests and wetlands. Their history is difficult to trace because they appear only briefly in early colonial records.

Moraughtacund.description: The Moraughtacund, also called Morattico, were an Indigenous people of the Northern Neck of Virginia, especially near present-day Lancaster County and the Rappahannock River. In 1608, Captain John Smith encountered the Moraughtacund during his Chesapeake explorations and came ashore near present-day Morattico. Smith shared a meal with the tribe and became involved in a peace arrangement between the Moraughtacund and nearby Rappahannock people. By the mid-1600s, the Moraughtacund had left the Morattico area and moved westward, as English settlement expanded into their homeland.
Moraughtacund.language: The Moraughtacund likely spoke an Eastern Algonquian language connected to other Native languages of the Chesapeake and Rappahannock River region. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Moraughtacund.culture: The Moraughtacund lived in a river-based landscape shaped by the Rappahannock River, creeks, forests and coastal resources. They hunted deer and wild turkey, fished, gathered oysters and used local waterways for travel and trade. Native paths in the area, including the Morattico Path, later became important travel routes for English settlers. Archaeological traces such as arrowheads, pottery fragments and burial sites reflect the long Indigenous presence in the Morattico region.

Mvskoke (Muscogee).description: The Muscogee, also known as the Mvskoke, Muscogee Creek or just Creek, and the Muscogee Creek Confederacy (pronounced [m?skó??l?i] in the Muscogee language), are a group of related Indigenous peoples of the Southeastern Woodlands in the United States. Their historical homelands are in what now comprises southern Tennessee, much of Alabama, western Georgia and parts of northern Florida. Most of the Muscogee people were forcibly removed to Indian Territory (now Oklahoma) by the federal government in the 1830s during the Trail of Tears.
Mvskoke (Muscogee).language: The Muscogee language is a Muskogean language spoken by Muscogee (Creek) and Seminole people, primarily in the US states of Oklahoma and Florida.
Mvskoke (Muscogee).culture: They were known for their rapid incorporation of modernity, developing a written language, transitioning to yeoman farming methods, and accepting European-Americans and African-Americans into their society. Muscogee people continue to preserve chaya and share a vibrant tribal identity through events such as annual festivals, stickball games, and language classes. Paleo-Indians in the Southeast were hunter-gatherers who pursued a wide range of animals, including megafauna, which became extinct following the end of the Pleistocene age.During the time known as the Woodland period, from 1000 BC to 1000 AD, locals developed pottery and small-scale horticulture of the Eastern Agricultural Complex.

Myaamia.description: The Myaamia, commonly known as the Miami, are a sovereign Indigenous nation headquartered in Miami, Oklahoma. Their name means “Downstream People,” and their homelands include parts of present-day Indiana, Illinois, Ohio, Wisconsin and Michigan. Myaamia history begins at Saakiiweeyonki, near where the St. Joseph River enters Lake Michigan, and later centers along the Wabash River Valley. In the 1800s, many Myaamia people were forcibly removed first to Kansas and later to Indian Territory, present-day Oklahoma. Today, the Miami Tribe of Oklahoma maintains its government, land base, businesses and cultural programs while continuing to honor all Myaamia homelands as Myaamionki, the “Place of the Myaamia.”
Myaamia.language: The Myaamia language is part of the Algonquian language family. Language revitalization is central to Myaamia identity, cultural education and the preservation of traditional knowledge.
Myaamia.culture: Myaamia culture is deeply tied to rivers, wetlands, prairies, woodlands and seasonal cycles. Historically, communities grew corn and vegetables, hunted deer, bison, elk and smaller animals, and gathered maple sap for sugar in early spring. Villages were often built near river confluences and portages, where people could farm, trade, travel and defend their communities. Today, the Miami Tribe continues to strengthen cultural knowledge, language learning, land stewardship and care for elders, youth and future generations.

Mánu: Yį Įsuwą (Catawba).description: The Catawba, also known as Issa, Essa or Iswä but most commonly Iswa ("people of the river"), are a federally recognized tribe of Native Americans, known as the Catawba Indian Nation. Their current lands are in South Carolina, on the Catawba River, near the city of Rock Hill. Their territory once extended into North Carolina, as well, and they still have legal claim to some parcels of land in that state. They were once considered one of the most powerful Southeastern tribes in the Carolina Piedmont, as well as one of the most powerful tribes in the South as a whole, with other, smaller tribes merging into the Catawba as their post-contact numbers dwindled due to the effects of colonization on the region.
Mánu: Yį Įsuwą (Catawba).language: Catawba is one of two Eastern Siouan languages of the eastern US, which together with the Western Siouan languages formed the Siouan language family. The last native, fluent speaker of Catawba was Samuel Taylor Blue, who died in 1959. The Catawba people are now working to revitalize and preserve the Catawba language.
Mánu: Yį Įsuwą (Catawba).culture: Early Catawbas lived in villages which were surrounded by a wooden palisade or wall. There was a large council house in the village as well as a sweat lodge, homes, and an open plaza for meetings, games, and dances. The homes were rounded on top and made of bark. The dwellings were small with extended families living in a single structure. Catawbas were farmers. They planted crops like corn and squash along the banks of the river. They also fished and hunted. The Catawbas were a large and powerful group and waged war with neighboring tribes, especially the Cherokee.

Nacotchtank (Anacostan).description: The Nacotchtank, also known as the Anacostan, were the earliest known Indigenous inhabitants of the area now known as Washington, D.C. Their main village, also called Nacotchtank, was located along the southeast side of the Anacostia River near the future site of Capitol Hill. The name is often interpreted as meaning “trading village,” reflecting the community’s important role in regional exchange. Captain John Smith visited a Nacotchtank village in 1608 during his exploration of the Potomac River. After European colonization, disease, conflict and displacement greatly reduced the Nacotchtank population, and many survivors likely merged with the Piscataway or other neighboring Native communities.
Nacotchtank (Anacostan).language: The Nacotchtank spoke an Algonquian language related to other Native languages of the Potomac and Chesapeake region. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Nacotchtank (Anacostan).culture: The Nacotchtank lived at a major crossroads where the Anacostia and Potomac rivers connected coastal and inland trade routes. Their community relied on fishing, hunting, gathering and farming in the fertile floodplains, growing crops such as corn, beans, squash and potatoes. The rivers provided seasonal fish runs, transportation and communication, while surrounding forests supplied deer, turkey, ducks, geese, nuts, plants and stone for tools. Their location made them important traders, linking Native communities from the Chesapeake, Piedmont and interior regions.

Nandtaughtacund.description: The Nandtaughtacund, also spelled Nantaughtacund or associated with the Nanzatico, were an Indigenous people of Virginia’s Tidewater region. Their homeland was along the Rappahannock River, especially in areas of present-day King George and Caroline counties. When Captain John Smith encountered them in 1608, they lived at Kerahocak, a large village reportedly covering about 15 acres. The Nandtaughtacund were part of Powhatan’s wider political world and served as an important northern community within that network. As English settlers expanded along the Rappahannock, the tribe was heavily displaced and diminished by warfare, disease, land loss and colonial labor systems.
Nandtaughtacund.language: The Nandtaughtacund language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of the Chesapeake and Rappahannock River region. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Nandtaughtacund.culture: Nandtaughtacund culture was shaped by the Rappahannock River, which supported farming, fishing, hunting, gathering, travel and trade. Their village of Kerahocak was an important settlement and meeting place within the northern part of Powhatan’s political network. Archaeological work at the Nanzattico Indian Town site has uncovered evidence of long Indigenous occupation in the area, with artifacts dating back thousands of years. Over time, survivors of Nandtaughtacund and nearby river communities likely merged with other Native groups. Today, many descendants of displaced peoples from this region are connected to the federally recognized Rappahannock Tribe.

Nansemond.description: The Nansemond Indian Nation is an Indigenous nation whose homeland is along the Nansemond River, a tributary of the James River in southeastern Virginia. The name Nansemond means “fishing point,” reflecting the tribe’s long relationship with the river. Nansemond people lived in settlements on both sides of the river, where they fished, harvested oysters, hunted and farmed fertile land. English colonization and the Anglo-Powhatan Wars displaced many Nansemond families from their ancestral lands, while others moved into nearby areas of Virginia and North Carolina. The tribe received Virginia state recognition in 1985 and federal recognition in 2018.
Nansemond.language: The Nansemond language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of the Chesapeake region. Like many Virginia Native languages, it was not fully preserved because of colonization, displacement and forced assimilation.
Nansemond.culture: Nansemond culture is rooted in the Nansemond River, family networks, farming, fishing, hunting and community survival. The tribe signed the Treaty of 1677, which recognized tributary tribes and reservation lands, though Nansemond people continued to face displacement and legal pressure. In the 1800s, Nansemond families built community institutions such as Indiana Methodist Church and an Indian school for Nansemond children. Despite racial reclassification and efforts to erase Native identity, Nansemond families preserved kinship, surnames, oral history and community ties. Today, the Nansemond Indian Nation continues cultural education, tribal governance and efforts to protect its history and homeland.

Natchez.description: Natchez, North American Indian tribe that inhabited the east side of the lower Mississippi River. When French colonizers first interacted with the Natchez in the early 18th century, the tribal population comprised about 6,000 individuals living in nine villages between the Yazoo and Pearl rivers near the site of the present-day city of Natchez, Miss. The Natchez are noted for being the only Mississippian culture with complex chiefdom characteristics to have survived long into the period of European colonization. In 1731, after several wars with the French, the Natchez were defeated. Most of the captured survivors were shipped to Saint-Domingue and sold by into slavery; others took refuge with other tribes.
Natchez.language: The Natchez language is generally considered a language isolate. As originally proposed by John Swanton in the early 20th century, some scholars believe that it may be related to the Muskogean languages.
Natchez.culture: The early ancestors of the Natchez hunted and gathered in forests and fished in creeks and rivers of southwest Mississippi. With the introduction of the bow and arrow and improved cultivation of maize (corn), beans, and squash, the natives began to settle into the more sedentary life of farming. The rich soils and climate of Mississippi created fertile ground for crop cultivation. It was during this period (800-1400 A.D.) that hundreds, or possibly thousands of elaborate earthworks (mounds) were constructed along the Mississippi River and tributaries.

Neusiok.description: The Neusiok were an Indigenous people of the Southeastern Wooodlands in present-day North Carolina. They were also known as the Neuse Indians. The Neusiok lived along the southern banks of the Neuse River, primarily in what are now Craven and Carteret counties. Their village, Chattooks, was near what is now New Bern, North Carolina. In the later colonial period the Indians of the same region were commonly known as Neuse Indians and had dwindled by the year 1700 to 15 warriors in two towns, Chattooka and Rouconk. They probably disappeared by incorporation with the Tuscarora.
Neusiok.language: Their language is unattested but may have been an Algonquian or Iroquoian language
Neusiok.culture: Some cultural traits of Southeastern Woodlands peoples. Social traits included having a matrilineal kinship system, exogamous marriage between clans, and organizing into settled villages and towns. Southeastern cultures relies on evidence from diverse sources, including artifacts, historical documents, ethnography, linguistics, folklore, and oral history. Many cultural traditions reported by the earliest European explorers, such as the use of ceremonial mounds, the heavy reliance on corn (maize), and the importance of social stratification in some areas, were clearly developed during the Mississippian culture period.

O-ga-xpa Ma-zhoⁿ (O-ga-xpa) (Quapaw).description: The O-ga-xpa, commonly known as the Quapaw, are an Indigenous people whose name is often translated as “Downstream People.” According to Quapaw oral history, their ancestors were once part of a larger Dhegiha group that later separated into the Quapaw, Osage, Omaha, Ponca and Kansa peoples. The Quapaw moved down the Mississippi River into present-day Arkansas, where they became closely connected to the lower Mississippi and Arkansas river valleys. Their name reflects this history of traveling downstream after separating from related nations. Today, the Quapaw Nation is a federally recognized tribe headquartered in Oklahoma.
O-ga-xpa Ma-zhoⁿ (O-ga-xpa) (Quapaw).language: The Quapaw language belongs to the Dhegiha Siouan language group. It is related to the languages of the Osage, Omaha, Ponca and Kansa peoples.
O-ga-xpa Ma-zhoⁿ (O-ga-xpa) (Quapaw).culture: Quapaw culture is rooted in river homelands, oral history, clan relationships and community continuity. Their villages were connected to the Mississippi and Arkansas river systems, which supported farming, fishing, hunting, travel and trade. The story of the grapevine rope breaking during a river crossing explains both the separation of related Dhegiha peoples and the Quapaw identity as those who went downstream. Today, the Quapaw Nation continues to preserve language, history, cultural knowledge and sovereignty for future generations.

Occohannock.description: The Occohannock, also spelled Accohannock, were an Indigenous people of the Eastern Shore region of Virginia and Maryland. Their territory included villages along the Chesapeake Bay, the Annemessex River near present-day Crisfield, Maryland, Accohannock Creek in Virginia and nearby Chesapeake Bay islands. The Occohannock were connected to the Accomac Confederation and included several related communities, such as the Onancock, Machipongo, Metomkin, Chincoteague, Pungoteague and others. Captain John Smith visited them in 1608, and by the late 1600s they had lost much of their former territory. Over time, many Occohannock people moved between the Eastern Shore and Pocomoke lands in Maryland, while others intermarried with Quaker settlers and blended into nearby communities.
Occohannock.language: The Occohannock spoke an Algonquian language connected to other Native languages of the Chesapeake Bay and Eastern Shore region. Their bands were also part of the wider Accomac Confederation.
Occohannock.culture: Occohannock culture was closely tied to the Chesapeake Bay, its rivers, creeks, islands and wetlands. They fished, harvested shellfish, trapped, hunted waterfowl and gathered resources from the bay and marshes. They also farmed crops such as corn and squash, while hunting deer, rabbit, squirrel, raccoon, bear and elk. Their history reflects both deep knowledge of Chesapeake waterways and the loss of land and traditional lifeways under colonial settlement.

Ofo.description: The Mosopelea, or Ofo, were a Siouan-speaking Native American people who historically lived near the upper Ohio River. In reaction to Iroquois Confederacy invasions to take control of hunting grounds in the late 17th century, they moved south to the lower Mississippi River. According to the 1684 French map of Jean-Baptiste-Louis Franquelin, the Mosopelea had eight villages just north of the Ohio River, between the Muskingum and Scioto rivers, within the present-day state of Ohio, corresponding with the heart of Ohio Hopewell country. In 1699, the Ofo/Mosopelea were referred by French Jesuits as the Houspé, and were encountered living among the Tunica. Around 1700, French travelers reported Ofo villages in Mississippi on the Yazoo River.
Ofo.language: The Ofo language was a language spoken by the Ofo people, also called the Mosopelea
Ofo.culture: Many cultural traditions reported by the earliest European explorers, such as the use of ceremonial mounds, the heavy reliance on corn (maize), and the importance of social stratification in some areas, were clearly developed during the Mississippian culture period. The Southeast was one of the more densely populated areas of native North America at the time of European contact. Most groups resided in the piedmont, where they took advantage of extensive game resources, wild plant foods, and an abundance of arable land. The peoples of south Florida were an exception, as they adjusted to an essentially subtropical maritime way of life.

Okchai.description: The Okchai are a Muscogee tribe. They formed part of the former Creek (Muscogee) Confederacy in Alabama, prior to their removal during the 1830s to the Indian Territory. The Muscogee, also known as the Mvskoke, Muscogee Creek or just Creek, and the Muscogee Creek Confederacy in the Muscogee language), are a group of related Indigenous peoples of the Southeastern Woodlands in the United States. Their historical homelands are in what now comprises southern Tennessee, much of Alabama, western Georgia and parts of northern Florida.
Okchai.language: The Muscogee language is a member of the Muskogean family
Okchai.culture: Muscogee culture has greatly evolved over the centuries, combining mostly European-American influences; however, interaction with Spain, France, and England greatly shaped it as well. They were known for their rapid incorporation of modernity, developing a written language, transitioning to yeoman farming methods, and accepting European-Americans and African-Americans into their society. Muscogee people continue to preserve chaya and share a vibrant tribal identity through events such as annual festivals, stickball games, and language classes. The Stomp Dance and Green Corn Ceremony are revered gatherings and rituals.

Onawmanient.description: The Onawmanient were a small Indigenous group associated with the lower Potomac River region and the broader Chesapeake Bay world. They appear in colonial-era records among Native communities living in present-day Virginia and Maryland. Like many smaller Chesapeake groups, the Onawmanient were affected by disease, warfare, English settlement and pressure on Native lands. Over time, they likely merged with neighboring Native communities and disappeared from records as a separate tribe.
Onawmanient.language: The Onawmanient likely spoke an Algonquian language connected to other Native languages of the Potomac and Chesapeake region. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Onawmanient.culture: The Onawmanient lived in a river-based environment where waterways supported food, travel, trade and communication. Their community likely relied on fishing, farming, hunting and gathering from the forests, wetlands and riverbanks around them. The Potomac connected them to nearby Native towns and regional exchange networks. Their history is difficult to trace because they were only briefly documented in colonial records, but their presence reflects the dense Indigenous landscape of the Chesapeake before and during early colonization.

Opiscopank.description: The Opiscopank were an Indigenous community associated with the coastal plain of present-day eastern North Carolina. They appear only briefly in historical records, so much of their history is difficult to trace. Their homeland was likely connected to the rivers, sounds, wetlands and forests that shaped Native life across the Carolina coastal region. Like many smaller Native communities in the area, the Opiscopank were affected by disease, colonial expansion, warfare and population movement. Over time, they likely merged with neighboring Native groups and disappeared from records as a separate tribe.
Opiscopank.language: The Opiscopank language was not well recorded. It may have been related to other Native languages of the Carolina coastal region, but its exact classification is uncertain.
Opiscopank.culture: The Opiscopank likely lived in a landscape shaped by waterways, marshes, forests and fertile lowlands. Their community probably relied on fishing, hunting, gathering and farming crops such as corn, beans and squash. Rivers and sounds would have supported travel, trade and connections with nearby Native towns. Because little was written about the Opiscopank, their history is best understood as part of the broader Indigenous world of eastern North Carolina.

Očhéthi Šakówiŋ.description: The Očhéthi Šakówiŋ, often translated as the Seven Council Fires, refers to the historic alliance of Dakota, Lakota and Nakota peoples. The seven council fires include the Mdewakanton, Wahpekute, Wahpeton, Sisseton, Yankton, Yanktonai and Teton/Lakota divisions. Together, they have also been known as the Sioux or Great Sioux Nation, though Očhéthi Šakówiŋ is the traditional name for this larger alliance. Today, Dakota, Lakota and Nakota nations have communities and governments across Minnesota, North Dakota, South Dakota, Nebraska, Montana and parts of Canada, including Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Alberta. Their history includes strong resistance to U.S. expansion, with leaders such as Tatanka Iyotanka, or Sitting Bull, remaining important figures of leadership and resilience.
Očhéthi Šakówiŋ.language: The Očhéthi Šakówiŋ peoples speak related dialects often identified as Dakota, Lakota and Nakota. These languages belong to the Siouan language family and remain central to cultural identity, ceremony and community education.
Očhéthi Šakówiŋ.culture: Očhéthi Šakówiŋ culture is rooted in kinship, land, language, ceremony, leadership and responsibility to the people. Communities historically lived across woodlands, river valleys and the northern Plains, with lifeways shaped by farming, hunting, gathering, trade and later Plains horse culture. The buffalo became especially central to food, shelter, tools, clothing and spiritual life for many Plains communities. Each council fire maintained its own identity while remaining connected through shared language, diplomacy and cultural values. Today, Očhéthi Šakówiŋ nations continue to uphold sovereignty, treaty rights, language revitalization, ceremony and land protection.

Pamlico (Pomouik).description: Pamlico, Algonquian-speaking Indians who lived along the Pamlico River in what is now Beaufort county, N.C., U.S., when first encountered by Europeans. These sedentary agriculturists were almost destroyed by smallpox in 1696, and in 1710 the 75 survivors lived in a single village. The Pamlico Indians lived south of the Pamlico River in present-day Beaufort and Pamlico Counties and were known as the Pomouik by members of the 1585-86 Raleigh expeditions. The Raleigh colonists referred to the Pamlico in 1585-86 by the name Pomoui.
Pamlico (Pomouik).culture: After the Tuscarora War, the Machapunga also were assigned to a reservation. Other groups on the Pamlico Sound joined either the Machapunga or the Tuscarora. With growing white presence in eastern Carolina, more products of European origin were introduced to the natives. Guns were regularly used instead of bows and arrows during the eighteenth century. Iron hatchets had likewise replaced wooden clubs. English clothes were also widely used by the natives. The Roanoke chief had an English-style house built in 1654. Other practices persisted through the nineteenth century, including the women making baskets of rushes and silk grass. They decorated them with woven-in life motifs.

Pamunkey.description: The Pamunkey Indian Tribe is an Indigenous nation whose homeland is along the Pamunkey River in present-day King William County, Virginia. The Pamunkey were one of the central tribes of Tsenacomoco, the political alliance led by Powhatan when English settlers arrived at Jamestown in 1607. The tribe has maintained a reservation on the Pamunkey River since the colonial period, making it one of the oldest reservations in the United States. Pamunkey leaders, including Cockacoeske, defended tribal lands and rights through treaties, petitions and diplomacy during the seventeenth century. The Pamunkey Tribe received Virginia state recognition in 1983 and federal recognition in 2015.
Pamunkey.language: The Pamunkey language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of tidewater Virginia. Like many Virginia Native languages, it was heavily disrupted by colonization and forced assimilation.
Pamunkey.culture: Pamunkey culture is deeply tied to the Pamunkey River, reservation land, pottery, fishing, hunting and tribal governance. The tribe has preserved pottery traditions since before the colonial era, and the Pamunkey Indian Museum helps share tribal history through tools, pottery and other cultural materials. Pamunkey people have also maintained a long treaty relationship with Virginia, including the annual tribute tradition of presenting fish and game to the governor. Today, the tribe continues to govern itself through an elected chief and council while protecting its land, history and cultural identity.

Pascagoula.description: The name Pascagoula is a Choctaw term meaning "bread eater". Choctaw native Americans using the name Pascagoula are named after the words for "bread eaters". In 1699 the Pascagoula Indians lived in southwestern Alabama and southeastern Mississippi, but in the middle eighteenth century they crossed the Mississippi and settled near the mouth of the Red River in Louisiana. In the early nineteenth century pressure from American settlers forced them farther westward. Some of the Pascagoulas entered Texas and lived with the Biloxi Indians near the Neches River in the area of present Angelina County, and others seem to have settled on the Red River in northeastern Texas.
Pascagoula.language: They spoke their own language which was different from neighboring languages in addition to Mobilian Jargon. Their language is undocumented.
Pascagoula.culture: The Mississippian period (AD 1000-1550) marked a new way of life for Native Americans in what is now the midwestern and southeastern United States. Prior to this time, people in those regions gathered wild foods and supplemented them with produce from small garden plots. Most communities were small. About 1,000 years ago, this older way of life changed as communities grew in size. Mississippian culture was not a single “tribe,” but many societies sharing a similar way of life or tradition. Mississippian peoples lived in fortified towns or small homesteads, grew corn, built large earthen mounds, maintained trade networks, had powerful leaders, and shared similar symbols and rituals.

Paspahegh.description: The Paspahegh were an Indigenous people whose homeland was along the James River in present-day Virginia, near the area where Jamestown was founded in 1607. They were part of Tsenacommacah, the Powhatan name for the broader homeland connected to Powhatan’s political world. Their village site, located only a few miles upstream from Jamestown, became one of the Native communities most directly affected by early English colonization. Contact with the English brought trade, conflict and violence, and the Paspahegh were eventually devastated by the expansion of the Jamestown colony.
Paspahegh.language: The Paspahegh language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of tidewater Virginia. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Paspahegh.culture: Paspahegh life was shaped by the James River, nearby forests, fields and wetlands. Their community included reed-covered houses, cooking areas, agricultural fields and ceremonial spaces. Paspahegh people hunted, fished, gathered wild foods, farmed crops, prepared meals, processed animal hides, made pottery, wove natural fibers and crafted dugout canoes. Archaeological evidence from Paspahegh village sites helps show how Native communities lived before and during the earliest years of English settlement in Virginia.

Pasquotank.description: The Pasquotank, also known as the Paspatank, were an Indigenous people of northeastern North Carolina. They lived along the Pasquotank River, north of Albemarle Sound, in what is now Pasquotank County. Their name is often interpreted from pask-e’-tan-ki, meaning “where the current divides or forks,” a fitting name for a homeland shaped by rivers and waterways. The Pasquotank were likely connected to the larger Weapemeoc group, along with nearby communities such as the Yeopim, Perquimans and Poteskeet. By the early 1700s, English writer John Lawson described them as a very small community, with only about 10 warriors remaining.
Pasquotank.language: The Pasquotank language was part of the Algonquian language family and was related to other coastal Carolina Native languages. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Pasquotank.culture: The Pasquotank lived in villages near rivers, sounds and fertile coastal lands. They farmed, hunted, fished and gathered food from the surrounding wetlands, forests and waterways. Some villages in the region were protected by palisades made of logs. The Pasquotank River and nearby Albemarle Sound supported travel, fishing, trade and communication with neighboring Native communities. Although the Pasquotank disappeared from records as a separate tribe, their name remains preserved in Pasquotank County and the Pasquotank River.

Patawomeck.description: The Patawomeck are an Indigenous people whose homeland is along the Potomac River in present-day Stafford and King George counties, Virginia. When English colonists settled Jamestown in 1607, the Patawomeck were a large tribe connected to Powhatan’s political world, but they often maintained their own diplomatic path. They supplied food such as corn to the Jamestown colonists during periods of starvation and later refused to join Opechancanough’s attacks against the English in 1622 and 1644. In the mid-1600s, conflict with colonists intensified, and after the death of Chief Wahanganoche, many Patawomeck people were killed, displaced or forced into servitude. Descendants later regrouped in the White Oak area of Stafford County, where many Patawomeck families remain connected today.
Patawomeck.language: The Patawomeck language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of the Chesapeake region. The English pronunciation of the tribal name as “Potomac” became the source of the name Potomac River.
Patawomeck.culture: Patawomeck culture is rooted in the Potomac River region, especially through farming, hunting, fishing and family continuity. Their ancestors passed down knowledge of agriculture, river life and traditional foodways across generations. Some Patawomeck descendants continue to preserve skills such as making intricate eel baskets, a practice with deep roots in the tribe’s fishing traditions. The tribe’s history is also closely tied to Pocahontas, who had family connections to the Patawomeck and was staying with Japasaw’s family when she was captured by the English. Today, Patawomeck identity continues through descendants, community memory and cultural preservation in Stafford County.

Pee Dee.description: The Pedee people, also Pee Dee and Peedee, were a historic Native American tribe of the Southeastern United States. Historically, their population has been concentrated in the Piedmont of present-day South Carolina. It is believed that in the 17th and 18th centuries, English colonists named the Pee Dee River and the Pee Dee region of South Carolina for the tribe. round 1550, the Pedee migrated from the lower Pee Dee River of the Atlantic Coastal Plain to the upper Pee Dee River of the Piedmont and remained there for about a century. They displaced local hill tribes, such as the Saponi, who resettled the region when the Pedee left.
Pee Dee.language: May have been a dialect of Catawba, but the Pedee language was extinct by the 19th century. No words from the language were recorded
Pee Dee.culture: The Pee Dee culture is an archaeological culture spanning 1000 to 1500 CE. It is divided into the Teal phase (1000–1200), Town Creek phase (1200–1400), and Leak phase (1400–1500). The Pee Dee were part of the South Appalachian Mississippian culture. Several structures, including some that served as burial or mortuary houses, were constructed around the edge of the plaza. The mound, plaza, and habitation zone were enclosed by a stockade made of closely set posts. Evidence of five episodes of stockade building has been found. All but the latest stockade stood before the mound was constructed.

Pensacola.description: The Pensacola were a Native American people who lived in the western part of what is now the Florida Panhandle and eastern Alabama for centuries before first contact with Europeans until early in the 18th century. They are the source of the name of Pensacola Bay and the city of Pensacola. They lived in the area until the mid-18th century, but were thereafter assimilated into other groups. The historical Pensacola people lived in part of a region once occupied by a group that archaeologists call the Pensacola culture, a regional variation of the Mississippian culture that lasted from 1100 to 1700 CE.
Pensacola.culture: The settlement pattern of the Pensacola culture area suggests that the area was a series of minor chiefdoms with their own local centers such as Fort Walton Mound with one large paramount chiefdom located at the Bottle Creek site. Unlike their Fort Walton neighbors to the east, Pensacola peoples relied more on the use of coastal resources than on maize agriculture. The Pensacola's first contact with Europeans may have been with the Narvaez expedition in 1528. While they had great farming success, Southeastern Native Americans also continued to hunt and fish. They hunted deer with bows and arrows and fished in rivers and in the Gulf of Mexico for protein. In southern Florida, Calusa people developed complex fishing and trapping systems for clams, mussels, and saltwater fish.

Perquimans.description: The Perquimans, historically spelled Perquiman, were an Indigenous people of northeastern North Carolina. Their homeland stretched along the northern edge of Albemarle Sound and the Perquimans River, in what is now Perquimans County. They were closely connected to the larger Weapemeoc/Yeopim communities of the Carolina coastal plain. In the 1660s, Perquimans leaders were involved in some of the earliest surviving land deeds in North Carolina with English and Welsh settlers. By the early 1700s, disease, displacement and colonial land pressure had greatly reduced their population, though their name remains preserved in Perquimans County and the Perquimans River.
Perquimans.language: The Perquimans language was part of the Algonquian language family and was related to other coastal Carolina Native languages. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Perquimans.culture: The Perquimans lived in a river-and-sound environment shaped by waterways, forests, wetlands and fertile coastal land. They farmed crops such as corn, beans and squash, while also hunting, fishing and gathering seasonal fruits, nuts and other wild foods. Their location along Albemarle Sound connected them to trade, travel and neighboring Native communities such as the Yeopim, Pasquotank and other Weapemeoc groups. Although the Perquimans disappeared from records as a separate tribe, descendants of Weapemeoc/Yeopim peoples and other regional Native communities continue to preserve Indigenous history in northeastern North Carolina.

Piankatank.description: The Piankatank were an Indigenous people of eastern Virginia whose homeland was along the Piankatank River, a waterway connected to present-day Mathews County and the Dragon Run region. Their history is closely tied to the broader Native world of tidewater Virginia and to neighboring communities such as the Kiskiack. In the late 1500s, the original Piankatank community was heavily disrupted and displaced by Powhatan, who then moved people from Kecoughtan into the area. By the mid-1600s, Kiskiack people had migrated to the Piankatank River and were granted a 5,000-acre reservation there by English colonists. The Piankatank name remains preserved in the river and local geography of eastern Virginia.
Piankatank.language: The Piankatank language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of tidewater Virginia. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Piankatank.culture: Piankatank culture was shaped by the river, forests, wetlands and coastal resources of eastern Virginia. The Piankatank River supported fishing, travel, trade and communication with nearby Native communities. People in the region likely farmed crops such as corn, beans and squash while also hunting, fishing and gathering seasonal foods. Their history reflects both the importance of waterways in Native life and the disruptions caused by regional conflict, Powhatan political expansion and later English colonization.

Piscataway.description: The Piscataway are an Indigenous people whose ancestral homeland extends across the western shore of the Chesapeake Bay and the Potomac River watershed, including much of present-day southern Maryland, Washington, D.C. and nearby areas of Virginia. Historically, the Piscataway were organized as a confederacy of tribes under the authority of a Tayac, or chief. Their capital, Moyaone, was located along the Potomac River and remains one of the most important Piscataway ancestral places. European contact began in the early 1600s, and colonization brought disease, warfare, broken treaties, land loss and forced displacement. Many Piscataway people remained in Maryland while others migrated north and became known as the Conoy; today, Piscataway communities continue to preserve their identity, history and relationship to their homelands.
Piscataway.language: The Piscataway language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of the Chesapeake region. Some early written records of the language were created by Jesuit missionaries, including religious translations, but the language was heavily disrupted by colonization and assimilation.
Piscataway.culture: Piscataway culture is rooted in the Potomac River, Chesapeake Bay, family networks, agriculture, fishing, ceremony and ancestral memory. Piscataway people farmed corn, beans and squash, fished and crabbed in the region’s rivers and tributaries, hunted, gathered plants and lived in longhouses.
