They maintained seasonal festivals, dugout canoe traditions, decorative arts and spiritual practices tied to ancestors and the land. Despite centuries of racial reclassification and efforts to erase Native identity, Piscataway families preserved community ties through kinship, churches, oral history and cultural gatherings. Today, Piscataway leaders and community members continue work around state recognition, repatriation of ancestors, land protection, environmental stewardship and public education.

Pissaseck.description: The Pissaseck were an Indigenous people who lived along the Rappahannock River in present-day Westmoreland County, Virginia, including the area around modern Leedstown. Captain John Smith included Pissaseck on his 1612 map of Virginia, marking it as part of the Indigenous landscape of the Rappahannock River region. The people of Pissaseck may have been closely connected to the Rappahannock, or they may have operated as a smaller independent community. By the mid-1600s, people living at Pissaseck and nearby communities appear to have merged with the Rappahannock or other neighboring Native groups as colonial pressure increased.
Pissaseck.language: The Pissaseck language came from the Virginia Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of the Rappahannock River and Chesapeake region. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Pissaseck.culture: The Pissaseck lived in a river-based environment where farming, hunting, fishing and gathering supported daily life. Their location along the Rappahannock River connected them to travel, trade and communication with nearby Native towns. As tributaries within Powhatan’s wider political world, they were part of a larger network of communities across Virginia’s coastal plain. Their history is closely tied to the Rappahannock River landscape, where Native towns, paths and settlement sites shaped the region long before English colonization.

Pocomoke-Assateague.description: The Pocomoke-Assateague were Indigenous peoples of the Eastern Shore whose homelands included the Pocomoke, Annemessex and Manokin rivers, the bay of Chincoteague, and areas of present-day Somerset, Worcester and Wicomico counties in Maryland, northern Accomack County in Virginia and nearby parts of Delaware. Captain John Smith’s 1612 map showed a Pocomoke king’s house on the Wighco Flu, now called the Pocomoke River. Their villages were located near rivers, creeks, bays and coastal islands, giving them access to seafood, travel routes and trade networks. As European settlement expanded, Pocomoke and Assateague people were pushed onto reservations such as Askiminokansen near present-day Snow Hill, Maryland. Despite land loss, treaties and colonial pressure, Pocomoke descendants continued living in rural Eastern Shore communities and preserving their heritage.
Pocomoke-Assateague.language: The Pocomoke and Assateague languages were part of the Algonquian language family. Captain John Smith noted that the Pocomoke and Nanticoke spoke a dialect different from the Accomac and Accohannock to the south, suggesting close ties with northern neighboring peoples such as the Nanticoke and Lenape.
Pocomoke-Assateague.culture: Pocomoke-Assateague culture was shaped by the rivers, marshes, forests, bays and barrier islands of the Eastern Shore. Women and children tended gardens of corn, beans and squash, while men and older boys hunted, trapped and gathered food from inland forests and waterways. The Pocomoke made wampum and peake from shell resources and traded these items for copper, stone, dyes and other goods. They also built dugout canoes from local cypress, cedar and pine, and lived in wigwams made from bent saplings covered with mats, thatch or bark. Seasonal traditions included planting, fishing, harvesting, hunting, gathering berries and nuts, honoring the dead and giving names within the community.

Poteskeet.description: The Weapemeoc Indians, also known as the Yeopim Indians, were a branch of Algonquian-speaking peoples living in sprawling villages along the northern rim of the Albemarle Sound when the Roanoke Island colonists arrived in the 1580s. The subtribes of the Weapemeoc included the Pasquotank, Perquimans, and Poteskeet. Although the Poteskeet’s main village was located on the mainland, they used the northern Outer Banks, including the area now within the Reserve, as hunting and fishing grounds. Oyster shell middens and pottery fragments found at several locations in the northern Outer Banks are evidence that the Poteskeet used this area.
Poteskeet.culture: They were partly agricultural and raised several varieties of beans (pulse), melons, and gourds, as well as corn, squash, and other vegetables. They were also food-gatherers, hunting turkeys and deer. During the summer months, fish, shellfish, raspberries, strawberries, walnuts, hickory nuts, and acorns supplemented their diet. For the most part, women tended the fields while men hunted, fished, and engaged in trade with one another, as well as with other groups to the north and west.

Powhatan.description: The Powhatan were Indigenous peoples of eastern Virginia whose homelands stretched across much of tidewater Virginia, from the James River north toward the Potomac River and parts of the Eastern Shore. Their broader homeland was called Tsenacommacah, and by 1607 it included more than 30 tribes under the leadership of Wahunsunacock, the paramount chief whom the English called Powhatan. Each tribe had its own local leaders, known as weroances or weroansquas, but many paid tribute to Powhatan in the form of corn, shell beads, copper, deerskins and other goods in exchange for protection. English colonization brought disease, warfare, land loss and pressure to assimilate, especially after the Anglo-Powhatan Wars and later treaties in 1646 and 1677. Despite centuries of disruption, several Virginia Native nations today trace their histories to the Powhatan world.
Powhatan.language: The Powhatan peoples spoke languages in the Algonquian language family. Some English words still used today, including moccasin, tomahawk, opossum and raccoon, come from Native languages of this region.
Powhatan.culture: Powhatan communities lived in villages near rivers and major tributaries, where waterways supported food, travel and trade. Their houses, called yehakins, were made from saplings covered with bark or reed mats. Powhatan people followed seasonal cycles of planting, hunting, fishing and gathering, growing corn, beans and squash while also eating fish, oysters, clams, berries, nuts, deer, turkey and other game. Men often hunted, fished, made tools and cleared land, while women farmed, gathered firewood, made clothing and prepared food. Clothing, tools, ornaments and body paint came from local materials such as deerskin, shells, copper, freshwater pearls, plant dyes, oils and animal fats.

Quiyoughcohannock.description: The Quiyoughcohannock were an Indigenous people whose homeland was along the James River in present-day Surry County, Virginia. They were one of the first Native communities encountered by the English after the Jamestown colonists arrived in 1607. Their principal village was located upriver from Mount Pleasant near present-day Claremont, and early records describe their leader, Pepsicuminah, as maintaining friendly relations with the English for a time. The Quiyoughcohannock helped guide Englishmen Nathaniel Powell and Anas Todkill southward in an unsuccessful search for survivors of the Roanoke Colony. As English settlement expanded along the James River, the Quiyoughcohannock were eventually overwhelmed by land loss, disease and conflict, and by the 1630s they had disappeared from the area as a distinct community.
Quiyoughcohannock.language: The Quiyoughcohannock language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of tidewater Virginia. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Quiyoughcohannock.culture: The Quiyoughcohannock lived in settled river communities surrounded by paths, springs, cornfields and wooded areas. Early English accounts describe their villages as having large cornfields and organized leadership, with the weroance receiving visitors in a formal and ceremonial way. The English also observed part of the huskanaw, a ritual initiation into manhood, at a Quiyoughcohannock village in 1608. Archaeology near Mount Pleasant has uncovered pottery and posthole patterns from Native longhouses, helping show the depth of Indigenous occupation along the James River before and during the early years of English settlement.

Rappahannock.description: The Rappahannock are an Indigenous people whose homeland is along the Rappahannock River in eastern Virginia. They first encountered an English ship around 1603, when an English captain killed a Rappahannock chief and took several tribal members to England. In 1607, Captain John Smith was brought as a prisoner to the Rappahannock capital, Topahanocke, where the people determined he was not the same Englishman responsible for that earlier attack. Smith returned in 1608 and mapped fourteen Rappahannock towns along the river. After centuries of displacement, land loss and racial reclassification, the Rappahannock Tribe maintained its identity, received Virginia state recognition in 1983 and federal recognition in 2018.
Rappahannock.language: The Rappahannock language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of the Chesapeake region. Like many Virginia Native languages, it was not fully preserved because of colonization, displacement and forced assimilation.
Rappahannock.culture: Rappahannock culture is rooted in the river that carries the tribe’s name. Their ancestors lived in towns and villages along the Rappahannock River, farming, fishing, hunting, gathering and using the river for travel and trade. The north side of the river held many towns, while the south side served as important hunting grounds. In the twentieth century, the tribe strengthened its community through incorporation in 1921, the founding of Rappahannock Indian Baptist Church in 1964 and later cultural programs. Today, the Rappahannock continue to share their history through tribal leadership, cultural education, powwows, dance, drum traditions and efforts to reconnect with ancestral river lands.

Roanoke.description: The Roanoke, also spelled Roanoac, were a people whose territory comprised present-day Dare County, Roanoke Island, and part of the mainland at the time of English exploration and colonization. They were one of the numerous Carolina Algonquian tribes, which may have numbered 5,000 to 10,000 people in total in eastern North Carolina at the time of English encounter. The last tribal chief of the Roanoke was believed to be Wanchese, who traveled to England with colonists in 1584. However, he may have just been an influential individual.
Roanoke.culture: Southeastern cultures relies on evidence from diverse sources, including artifacts, historical documents, ethnography, linguistics, folklore, and oral history. Many cultural traditions reported by the earliest European explorers, such as the use of ceremonial mounds, the heavy reliance on corn (maize), and the importance of social stratification in some areas, were clearly developed during the Mississippian culture period (c. 700–1600 ce). The Mississippians maintained fine craft traditions and also engaged in long-distance trade throughout the Southeast and the surrounding culture areas.

Saluda.description: The meaning of the name Saluda is unknown. Uncertain and circumstantial evidence indicates strongly that the Saluda were a band of Shawnee, and therefore of the Algonquian stock. The Saluda group primarily lived on the Saluda River in present-day South Carolina. As bands of Shawnee were moving into just that region from time to time during the period indicated, there is reason to think that this was one of them, all the more that a "Savana" creek appears on the same map flowing into Congaree River just below the Saluda settlement.
Saluda.culture: During the summer they lived in bark-covered houses. Their large villages were located near the fields in which women cultivated corn (maize) and other vegetables. The primary male occupation was hunting. In winter village residents dispersed to family hunting camps. Each village had a large council house that was also used for such religious ceremonies as the ritual purification of warriors. Other important ceremonies included the spring Bread Dance, held when the fields were planted; the Green Corn Dance, marking the ripening of crops; and the autumn Bread Dance.

Santee.description: The Santee were a historic tribe of Native Americans that once lived in South Carolina within the counties of Clarendon and Orangeburg, along the Santee River. The Santee were a small tribe even during the early eighteenth century and were primarily centered in the area of the present-day town of Santee, South Carolina. In South Carolina, there was a small Catawban tribe known as Santee, whose name came from the word for a river in their language, Santa. The Santee people's earliest documented presence with a high degree of certainty can be traced back to the early seventeenth century. Their settlement along the Santee River has since been dammed and is now called Lake Marion.
Santee.culture: Their nation was characterized by a form of despotism, where the chief held absolute authority, including the power of life and death over his people. This authoritative practice was inherited hereditarily from the previous chief. Santee rulers were interred atop mounds constructed in accordance with their status within the tribe, with a roof supported by poles sheltering their graves from the elements. Native American mound next to Lake Marion (formerly Santee River). South Carolina is thought to be the easternmost expression of the Mississippian culture's moundbuilding. Upon the death of other tribe members, a different tradition was observed.

Saponi.description: The Saponi are a Native American tribe historically based in the Piedmont of North Carolina and Virginia. They were part of the Monacan confederacies. Saponi, Tutelo, and Yesang were collectively called the Nahyssan. The Cayuga adopted the Saponi into the League of the Haudenosaunee in 1753, and some Saponi descendants are part of the Cayuga Nation. At the time of European contact up to the early 18th century, the Saponi lived in present-day Virginia and North Carolina. Their settlements extended into the New River in West Virginia. In the 17th and 18th centuries, some Saponi settled along the Roanoke River, its tributary the Staunton River, and the Yadkin River.
Saponi.culture: They were an Eastern Siouan people with a matrilineal society. They had settled villages and built houses of post-and-pole frames with central hearths. In the 17th century, men wore breechclouts and women wore deerhide aprons. Important leaders, such as medicine men, wore feather cloaks. British explorer John Lawson wrote that the Saponi were governed by a headman, an elders' council, and, when necessary, a war chief. Historically, Saponi people hunted deer, bear, beaver, squirrel, turkey, and other fowl. They may have hunted woodland bison and elk. They fished in rivers and the Atlantic Ocean. They farmed maize, beans, and squash and harvested wild plants including various nuts, berries, and stone fruits. Chiefs used staffs of hickory wood.

Secotan.description: The Secotans were one of several groups of Native Americans dominant in the Carolina sound region, between 1584 and 1590, with which English colonists had varying degrees of contact. Secotan villages included the Secotan, Aquascogoc, Dasamongueponke, Pomeiock (Pamlico) and Roanoac. In the Carolinas, colonization did not exist as a straight-line transition, from Native American to European rule. A rivalry marked the relationship between the two European powers, the English and the Spanish. Rivalries also existed between the Native American groups. Additionally, the Europeans often found themselves caught in the middle of conflicts, which existed between Native American groups.
Secotan.culture: An agricultural people, they relied on the crops they raised. Their proximity to the waters of the Albemarle and Pamlico Sounds connected them to a diverse local trade network. Their villages consisted of long houses that were covered in bark and woven mats, a charnel house, a place for prayer/religious ceremonies, a dance ground, tobacco and corn fields, and other fields with assorted crops. he Secotan Indians had a system of ranking within the people of the tribe. Chiefs and Priests of these people were better dressed than the people who wore less clothing such as breech cloth or deerskin aprons or skirts with an added cape in the winter. They believed in decorating their bodies with paint, tattoos and jewelry.

Sekakawon.description: The Sekakawon, also spelled Secacawoni or Chickacoan, were an Indigenous people of the lower Northern Neck region of Virginia. Their homeland was located near the Coan River and Potomac River, close to where those waterways meet the Chesapeake Bay. The region between the Rappahannock and Potomac rivers was once known as the Chickacoan Indian district. As English settlement expanded in the 1600s, Sekakawon lands were increasingly taken over by colonists. In 1655, colonial authorities assigned the Sekakawon and Wicocomico a shared reservation on Dividing Creek, but the Sekakawon largely left the area by 1659 rather than remain on land they had been forced to share.
Sekakawon.language: The Sekakawon language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of the Chesapeake region. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Sekakawon.culture: Sekakawon culture was shaped by the rivers, forests, wetlands and coastal plain of Virginia’s Northern Neck. Their community relied on fishing, farming, hunting and gathering from the rich lands and waterways between the Potomac and Rappahannock rivers. One important leader, Machywap Taptico, negotiated land exchanges with early English settlers as colonial pressure increased. Although colonial records later treated many local Native groups as “extinct,” Sekakawon history reflects a broader pattern of Native persistence, adaptation and merging with neighboring communities.

Seminole.description: The Seminole are a Native American people who developed in Florida in the 18th century. Today, they live in Oklahoma and Florida, and comprise three federally recognized tribes: the Seminole Nation of Oklahoma, the Seminole Tribe of Florida, and the Miccosukee Tribe of Indians of Florida, as well as independent groups. The Seminole people emerged in a process of ethnogenesis from various Native American groups who settled in Spanish Florida beginning in the early 1700s, most significantly northern Muscogee Creeks from what are now Georgia and Alabama.
Seminole.culture: The Seminoles located their new villages in the Everglades, a patchwork of dense thickets and wetlands that provided protective isolation from outsiders. The Seminoles generally welcomed those newcomers. Their economy emphasized hunting, fishing, and gathering wild foods such as nuts and berries; they also grew corn (maize), beans, squash, melons, and other produce on high ground within the wetlands. Homes included substantial log cabins and, later, thatched-roof shelters with open sides known as “chickees,” which promoted maximum ventilation. People typically wore long tunics; by the late 19th century, Seminole clothing was often decorated with brightly coloured strips of cloth.

Seminole Tribe of Florida (Big Cypress R.description: The Seminole Tribe of Florida’s Big Cypress Reservation is located in the Everglades of south Florida. Seminole people have lived in the Big Cypress area for generations, including during the Seminole War period, when leaders such as Abiaka, also known as Sam Jones, established camps there. Land that became part of the reservation was purchased and set aside in the late 1800s, and in 1911 President William Howard Taft formally allotted 17,000 acres for the reservation. Today, Big Cypress is one of the Tribe’s largest reservations and is home to Seminole families, cattle operations, cultural sites, the Ah-Tah-Thi-Ki Museum and a unique Everglades ecosystem.
Seminole Tribe of Florida (Big Cypress R.language: Mikasuki and Creek are important languages within Seminole communities, along with English.
Seminole Tribe of Florida (Big Cypress R.culture: Big Cypress is deeply tied to Seminole survival, cattle, community and the Everglades landscape. Seminole families lived in chickee camps, raised cattle, hunted, gathered and maintained traditions while resisting removal and adapting to changing conditions. In the 1940s, cattle became a major part of reservation life, with leaders such as Morgan Smith helping shape the cattle program that later influenced the Tribe’s modern government. The reservation is also an important center for cultural preservation through the Ah-Tah-Thi-Ki Museum, community events, patchwork, foodways, music, rodeos and storytelling. Today, Big Cypress continues to balance tradition, environmental stewardship and community growth.

Seminole Tribe of Florida (Brighton Rese.description: The Seminole Tribe of Florida’s Brighton Reservation is located near Lake Okeechobee in Glades County, Florida. Brighton has been an important center of Seminole ranching, education and community life. In the 1930s, Seminole leaders at Brighton helped build one of the Tribe’s first organized cattle programs, creating a foundation for economic independence and future tribal government. The reservation is also known for its long commitment to education, from the Brighton Indian Day School, which opened in 1939, to today’s Pemayetv Emahakv Charter School.
Seminole Tribe of Florida (Brighton Rese.language: Creek and Mikasuki are important languages within Seminole communities, along with English. At Brighton, language preservation is especially visible through school programs and Creek immersion efforts that teach children Seminole language and culture from an early age.
Seminole Tribe of Florida (Brighton Rese.culture: Brighton is strongly connected to Seminole cattle traditions and the history of Seminole cowkeepers. In the 1930s, leaders such as Frank Shore, Charlie Micco, Naha Tiger, Willie Gopher and Willie Tiger helped develop the Seminole Cattle Program, using both traditional knowledge and modern ranching practices. Education is also central to Brighton’s identity. The Brighton Indian Day School taught children academics while also supporting cultural learning, farming, cattle care and community values. Today, Pemayetv Emahakv Charter School continues that legacy by teaching Seminole language, history and culture alongside regular school subjects.

Seminole Tribe of Florida (Hollywood Res.description: The Seminole Tribe of Florida’s Hollywood Reservation, originally called the Dania Seminole Reservation, was established in 1926 in present-day Broward County, Florida. It became one of the Tribe’s major community centers as Seminole families moved there for safety, work and opportunity. Early residents such as Ada Tiger helped build the reservation’s future, and her children, Betty Mae Tiger Jumper and Howard Tiger, became important Seminole leaders. In 1957, Seminole citizens gathered under the Council Oak on the Hollywood Reservation to ratify the Tribe’s constitution and bylaws, helping establish the modern federally recognized Seminole Tribe of Florida.
Seminole Tribe of Florida (Hollywood Res.language: Mikasuki and Creek are both important languages within Seminole communities, along with English.
Seminole Tribe of Florida (Hollywood Res.culture: The Hollywood Reservation has long been a center of Seminole community life, leadership and economic development. Seminole families worked in agriculture, cattle, arts and crafts, tourism and cultural education while adapting to the growth of South Florida around them. In 1959, the Tribe opened Okalee Seminole Village, one of its first official business ventures and a place for sharing Seminole culture with visitors. The reservation is also tied to the rise of Indian gaming: the Tribe opened its high-stakes bingo hall there in 1979, a landmark step for tribal sovereignty and economic self-determination. Today, the Hollywood Reservation remains home to cultural events, the Council Oak, Okalee Seminole Village, the Seminole Hard Rock complex and community traditions that continue to connect Seminole people to their history.

Seminole Tribe of Florida (Immokalee Res.description: The Seminole Tribe of Florida’s Immokalee Reservation is located in Collier County, Florida. Although it officially became a reservation in 1989, Seminole history in the Immokalee area goes back much further. Seminole families used the dry, high ground around Immokalee during the wet season, and the area was once known as **Gopher Ridge** because of its many gopher tortoises. In the twentieth century, Seminole families built community there through camps, work, education and advocacy. Tribal leaders and community members, especially women such as Nancy Motlow, Louise Motlow and Elaine Aguilar, helped secure land for future generations.
Seminole Tribe of Florida (Immokalee Res.language: The name Immokalee comes from the Mikasuki word "I-mok-a-li", meaning “my home.” Mikasuki and Creek are both important languages within Seminole communities, though English is also widely spoken today.
Seminole Tribe of Florida (Immokalee Res.culture: Seminole life in Immokalee has been shaped by family, work, cattle, chickee camps, traditional clothing, foodways and community leadership. Many families once lived in chickees without running water or electricity, while emphasizing education, self-reliance and a strong work ethic. The community continues to honor Seminole traditions through rodeos, dance, patchwork clothing, cooking, storytelling and cultural gatherings. Today, the Immokalee Reservation also includes important community resources, businesses, a casino hotel, trading post and expanding health and public safety services that support Seminole families and the wider area.

Seminole Tribe of Florida (Tampa Reserva.description: The Seminole Tribe of Florida’s Tampa Reservation was established in 1980, when nine acres of land off Orient Road were placed into trust for the Tribe. Although the reservation is relatively recent, Seminole people and their ancestors have long-standing ties to the Tampa Bay region and central Florida. The Tampa community began with modest buildings, including a bingo hall, gift shop, museum, smoke shop and Bobby’s Seminole Village. Over time, the reservation became a major center of Seminole economic development through the Seminole Hard Rock Hotel & Casino Tampa.
Seminole Tribe of Florida (Tampa Reserva.language: Mikasuki and Creek are important languages within Seminole communities, along with English. These languages carry cultural teachings, family histories, place names and community knowledge.
Seminole Tribe of Florida (Tampa Reserva.culture: The Tampa Reservation’s history is closely tied to family leadership, sacrifice and economic self-determination. Ruby Tiger Osceola, a respected Seminole matriarch, helped form the early Tampa community when she moved there with members of her family. The reservation grew from a small bingo operation into one of the Tribe’s most important economic centers, supporting services and opportunities across Seminole communities. Although casino expansion displaced some Tampa families, the Tribe later purchased land near Lakeland to help rebuild community life and provide homes for those families. Today, Tampa represents both Seminole resilience and the Tribe’s role in shaping modern tribal sovereignty through business, culture and community planning.

Sewee.description: The Sewee or "Islanders" were a Native American tribe that lived in present-day South Carolina in North America. Their territory was on the lower course of the Santee River and the coast westward to the divide of Ashley River, around present-day Moncks Corner, South Carolina. Ethnologist John Reed Swanton estimated there were 800 Sewee in 1600. In 1670, the English founded the coastal town of Charleston in the Carolina Colony on land belonging to the Etiwan people and neighboring tribes like the Sewee. Sewee and other native peoples began participating in the Deerskin trade shortly thereafter.
Sewee.language: No words of their language have survived, but the Sewee are regarded as Siouan on strong circumstantial grounds
Sewee.culture: The Sewee hunted, processed, and exchanged deer hides for manufactured goods and glass beads from the English. However, they felt that English traders had become middlemen. Noting that the English ships always landed at the same location, the Sewee believed that by rowing to the point on the horizon where the ships first appeared, they could reach England and establish better trading prices. Therefore, the Sewee nation decided to construct canoes with woven mat sails for their expedition.

Shakori.description: Shakori is a native name but its significance unknown, though perhaps the same as Sugari, "stingy or spoiled people," or "of the river whose-water-cannot-be drunk." The Shakori belonged to the Siouan linguistic family, their closest connections being evidently with the southern division of the Siouan tribes of the East. Colonel John Barnwell identified them with the Sissipahaw. The Shakori were an indigenous people of the Southeastern Woodlands. They were thought to be a Siouan people, closely allied with other nearby tribes such as the Eno and the Sissipahaw. As their name is also recorded as Shaccoree, they can be confused with the Sugaree, but the latter are Catawba people.
Shakori.culture: Although little is known about the Shakori, at the time of contact, they were not noted as being noticeably different from the surrounding tribes. They made their wigwams and other structures out of interwoven saplings and sticks; these were covered in mud as opposed to the bark typically used by other nearby tribes. They were described as being similar to traditional dwellings of the Quapaw from Arkansas. In the center of the village, men often played a slinging stone game, probably similar to the chunkey played by tribes further south and west.

Shawandasse Tula (Shawanwaki/Shawnee).description: The Shawandasse Tula, also known as the Shawanwaki or Shawnee, resided on the greater Ohio River Valley, including parts of present-day Ohio, Kentucky, Indiana, West Virginia and Pennsylvania. Their name is often connected to an Algonquian word meaning “southerner.” In the 1600s, warfare, disease and Haudenosaunee pressure forced many Shawnee communities to scatter across a wide region, though many later returned to the Ohio Valley. Continued colonial expansion, warfare and U.S. removal policies eventually divided Shawnee people into three federally recognized nations based in Oklahoma: the Shawnee Tribe, the Absentee Shawnee Tribe and the Eastern Shawnee Tribe.
Shawandasse Tula (Shawanwaki/Shawnee).language: The Shawnee language is part of the Algonquian language family. It is related to the languages of peoples such as the Fox, Kickapoo and Sauk, and remains an important part of Shawnee cultural identity today.
Shawandasse Tula (Shawanwaki/Shawnee).culture: Shawnee communities traditionally lived in large villages near farm fields during the warmer months, where women grew crops such as corn, beans and squash. Men hunted, and in winter families often moved to smaller hunting camps. Shawnee towns included council houses used for political gatherings and ceremonies. Important ceremonies included the Bread Dance, Green Corn Dance and other seasonal gatherings tied to planting, harvest, renewal and community balance. Shawnee society included five major divisions and clans, with civil leaders often inheriting their roles and war leaders chosen for bravery, skill and experience.

Sissipahaw.description: The Sissipahaw or Haw were a Native American tribe of North Carolina. They are also variously recorded as Saxahapaw, Sauxpa, Sissipahaus. Their settlements were generally located in the vicinity of modern-day Saxapahaw, North Carolina on the Haw River in Alamance County upstream from Cape Fear. They are possibly first recorded by the Spaniard Vendera in the 16th century as the Sauxpa in South Carolina. Their last mention in history is that the tribe joined the Yamasee against the English colonists in the Yamasee War of 1715.
Sissipahaw.language: Siouan linguistic family, their language is extinct, with no words being known.
Sissipahaw.culture: Very little is known of the Sissipahaw, aside from a few notes in history. Archaeological evidence from Alamance County indicates that the Sissipahaw, much like the Shakori, lived in wigwam-like structures, farmed corn and beans, and hunted the woods for turkey, venison, and bear. The tribe was closely connected with the Shakori, of which they may have been a band or closely related and allied tribe.

Skaruhreh/Tuscarora (North Carolina).description: The Skaruhreh, commonly known as the Tuscarora, are a Indigenous tribe whose homelands included the coastal plain and river systems of present-day North Carolina and Virginia. Historically, Tuscarora towns extended across eastern and southeastern North Carolina, including areas near the Roanoke, Neuse, Pamlico and Cape Fear rivers. In the early 1700s, colonial land theft, enslavement, disease and violence led to the Tuscarora War. Afterward, many Tuscarora migrated north and joined the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, becoming the Sixth Nation, while others remained in North Carolina and continued living in tribal communities.
Skaruhreh/Tuscarora (North Carolina).language: The Tuscarora language is part of the Iroquoian language family, related to Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga and Seneca. The name Skaruhreh is often translated as “hemp gatherers” or “people of the shirt.”
Skaruhreh/Tuscarora (North Carolina).culture: Tuscarora communities were organized through towns, clans, councils and strong kinship networks. They farmed crops such as corn, beans and squash, hunted, fished and traded widely across the eastern woodlands. Tuscarora society is traditionally matrilineal, with children belonging to their mother’s clan. Cultural life includes longhouse traditions, clan responsibilities, ceremonies such as Green Corn and Midwinter, and teachings centered on maintaining a good mind, a good heart and responsibility to community. Today, Tuscarora people continue to preserve language, sovereignty, traditional food systems, ceremonies and Haudenosaunee teachings in North Carolina, New York and Canada.

Sugaree.description: The Sugaree, also spelled Sugeree or Suturee, were a small Indigenous tribe of the Carolina Piedmont. They lived mainly along Sugar Creek, in what is now York County, South Carolina, and Mecklenburg County, North Carolina. Their villages were located near the Waxhaw and Catawba peoples, in a fertile region connected to the Catawba River system. The Sugaree are rarely mentioned in early colonial records before the early 1700s. They were likely weakened by disease, colonial pressure and the Yamasee War of 1715, and later merged with the Catawba.
Sugaree.language: No Sugaree words were preserved in written records. Scholars generally believe they spoke a Siouan language closely related to Catawba and possibly Shakori.
Sugaree.culture: The Sugaree lived in multiple towns and settlements near Sugar Creek and the Catawba River. Their homeland provided fertile soil for farming, along with forests and waterways for hunting, gathering and fishing. Like neighboring Piedmont Siouan peoples, they likely grew crops such as corn, beans and squash while also relying on deer, fish, nuts and wild plants. Although the Sugaree disappeared as a separate tribe, their name remains preserved in Sugar Creek, which flows through North and South Carolina.

S’atsoyaha (Yuchi).description: The Yuchi people, also spelled Euchee and Uchee, are a Native American tribe based in Oklahoma. Their original homeland was in the southeast of the present United States. In the 16th century, Yuchi people lived in the eastern Tennessee River valley in Tennessee. In the late 17th century, they moved south to Alabama, Georgia, and South Carolina. After suffering many fatalities from epidemic disease and warfare in the 18th century, several surviving Yuchi bands were removed to Indian Territory in the 1830s, together with their allies the Muscogee Creek.
S’atsoyaha (Yuchi).language: Yuchi is classified as a language isolate, because it is not known to be related to any other language.
S’atsoyaha (Yuchi).culture: They relied on intensive hoe agriculture of corn, beans, and squash, and hunting of white-tailed deer, bear, and elk. Before 1715 the Yuchis lived in permanent towns which were considered either red (war) or white (peace). Each town typically contained a square ground, a hot house, and a ball field, as well as one-room domestic structures. The square ground was the focus of male daily life, and it surrounded the sacred fire. The Busk, or Green Corn Ceremony, was the ceremonial focus of the year.

Taensa.description: The Taensa (also Taënsas, Tensas, Tensaw) were a Native American people whose settlements at the time of European contact in the late 17th century were located in present-day Tensas Parish, Louisiana. They numbered about 100 persons in 1805. They later moved south to Bayou Boeuf and later still to Grand Lake, "after which the remnant disappeared from history." The Taensa and the closely related Natchez are descendants of the late prehistoric Plaquemine culture (1200-1700 CE). The Plaquemine culture was a Mississippian culture variant centered on the Lower Mississippi River valley.
Taensa.culture: As such their languages, political, religious, and material cultures were very similar to the Natchez. Chiefs exercised absolute power and were treated with great respect; unlike more egalitarian customs among the northern tribes the early chroniclers were used to. The Taensa were sedentary maize growing agriculturalists as opposed to hunter gatherers and lived in permanent villages with wattle and daub buildings. Like other Native Americans in the southeast it also had an open plaza area used for public rituals and functions such as the Green Corn Ceremony and games such as chunkey and the ballgame.

Tayoroniku (Tunica).description: The Tunica people are a group of linguistically and culturally related Native American tribes in the Mississippi River Valley, which include the Tunica (also spelled Tonica, Tonnica, and Thonnica); the Yazoo; the Koroa (Akoroa, Courouais); and possibly the Tioux. Over the next centuries, under pressure from hostile neighbors, the Tunica migrated south from the Central Mississippi Valley to the Lower Mississippi Valley. Eventually they moved westward and settled around present-day Marksville, Louisiana.
Tayoroniku (Tunica).culture: By the Middle Mississippian period, local Late Woodland peoples in the Central Mississippi Valley had developed or adopted a full Mississippian lifestyle, with intensive maize agriculture, hierarchical political structures, mussel shell-tempered pottery and participation in the Southeastern Ceremonial Complex. Over the next centuries, settlement patterns changed to a pattern of more centralized towns, with defensive palisades and ditches, indicating a state of endemic warfare had developed between local competing polities. Material culture, such as pottery styles and mortuary practices, began to diverge at this point.

Taíno.description: The Taíno are Indigenous Peoples of the Caribbean. At the time of European contact in 1492, Taíno communities lived across much of Kuba/Cuba, Borikén/Puerto Rico, Hispaniola/Haiti and the Dominican Republic, Jamaica, the Bahamas, the northern Lesser Antilles and parts of southern Florida. They were the first Indigenous Peoples of the Western Hemisphere encountered by Christopher Columbus and the first to be called “Indians” by Europeans. Spanish colonization, forced labor, disease and violence caused a devastating population decline, but Taíno descendants and communities continue to reclaim and preserve Taíno identity, history and culture today.
Taíno.language: The Taíno language was part of the Arawakan language family, with influences from other Caribbean languages. Many Taíno words remain in common use today, including canoe, hammock, tobacco, hurricane and barbecue.
Taíno.culture: Taíno communities lived in towns and villages across the Caribbean and developed strong systems of farming, fishing, art, pottery, weaving and medicine. They grew crops such as yucca/cassava, corn, beans and sweet potatoes, and they developed ways to process yucca safely for food. Taíno people also had deep knowledge of plants used for healing, tools, dyes and other daily needs. Today, Taíno heritage continues through language recovery, family histories, agriculture, ceremonies, cultural organizations, archaeological sites and renewed efforts to teach Taíno history throughout the Caribbean.

Tequesta.description: The Tequesta, also Tekesta, Tegesta, Chequesta, Vizcaynos, were a Native American tribe. At the time of first European contact they occupied an area along the southeastern Atlantic coast of Florida. They had infrequent contact with Europeans and had largely migrated by the middle of the 18th century. The Tequesta lived in the southeastern parts of present-day Florida. They lived in the region since the 3rd century BC in the late Archaic period of the continent, and remained for roughly 2,000 years, By the 1800s, most had died as a result of settlement battles, slavery, and disease.
Tequesta.culture: They relied mainly on fish, shellfish, nuts, and berries for food. The men caught sharks, sailfish, sea cows, and porpoises in the waters of Biscayne Bay and the Miami River, while the women and children collected clams, conchs, oysters, and turtle eggs in the shallow waters. The manatee was considered a delicacy and served mainly to the chiefs and other prominent leaders. The Tequesta also gathered palmetto berries, coco plums, sea grapes, and palm nuts to eat. In the Everglades, they hunted bear, deer, wild boar, and small mammals. The Tequesta used shells and sharks' teeth for a variety of tools. These included hammers, chisels, fishhooks, drinking cups, and spearheads. Sharks' teeth were used to carve out logs to make canoes.

Timucua.description: The Timucua were a Native American people who lived in Northeast and North Central Florida and southeast Georgia. They were the largest indigenous group in that area and consisted of about 35 chiefdoms, many leading thousands of people. The various groups of Timucua spoke several dialects of the Timucua language. At the time of European contact, Timucuan speakers occupied about 19,200 square miles in the present-day states of Florida and Georgia, with an estimated population of 200,000. Milanich notes that the population density calculated from those figures.
Timucua.culture: The Timucua grew much of their own food and stayed in relatively the same places from year to year. Important crops to Timucua farmers were pumpkins, cucumbers, peas, gourds, maize, and beans, as well as other fruits and starches. The Timucua primarily gathered hickory nuts, berries, and acorns, which grew plentifully on vines and trees. They fished and hunted, as well as gathered things like Spanish moss from the nature around them. Fish and seafood, as their primary source of protein, were incredibly important to the Timucua diet. The Timucua were skilled at building canoes to catch fish.

Tioux.description: The Tioux, also spelled Tiou or Thysia, were a small Native tribe of present-day Mississippi. They lived mainly along the Yazoo River and were connected to the broader Natchez Confederacy. Early records mention the Tioux in the late 1600s, when they were living in permanent villages near other Native communities of the lower Mississippi Valley. Their population declined sharply because of European-introduced diseases, conflict with neighboring tribes and displacement from their homelands. By the early 1700s, many Tioux survivors had joined the Natchez, Bayogoula, Acolapissa or other nearby groups, and the Tioux no longer continued as a separate tribe.
Tioux.language: The Tioux language was never fully recorded. Some sources suggest it was different from Natchez and may have been related to the Tunica language family, since the Tioux were closely connected with other Tunican-speaking groups in the lower Mississippi Valley.
Tioux.culture: The Tioux lived in settled villages and practiced farming as a major part of daily life. They grew crops such as corn, beans and squash, while also hunting, trapping, fishing and gathering roots, nuts and seeds. Their location along the Yazoo River connected them to trade, travel and food resources in the lower Mississippi Valley. As part of the Natchez Confederacy, the Tioux were linked to a wider world of river communities, alliances and ceremonial traditions.

Tocobaga.description: Tocobaga (occasionally Tocopaca) was the name of a chiefdom, its chief, and its principal town during the 16th century. The chiefdom was centered around the northern end of Old Tampa Bay, the arm of Tampa Bay that extends between the present-day city of Tampa and northern Pinellas County. n approximately 1528, Pánfilo de Narváez, a Spanish explorer, arrived in the Tampa Bay area. He and his men found the Tocobaga and brought disease and violence to the tribe's peaceful existence. As a result, the Tocobaga Indians became extinct within the next 100 years.
Tocobaga.culture: The Tocobaga Indians lived in small villages at the northern end of Tampa Bay from 900 to the 1500s. The Tocobaga Indians built mounds within their villages. The women of the Tocobaga tribes had a garbage heap called a midden, which was located next to their kitchen. Because of their proximity to both the bay and freshwater streams, the Tocobaga fished and gathered shellfish as their primary source of food. They also ate manatees, which were abundant in the nearby waters. During this time, the Tampa Bay area was rich with animals such as deer, rabbits, armadillo, and squirrels. As a result, the Tocobaga became great hunters.

Waccamaw (Woccon).description: Waccamaw Siouan Indians are one of eight state-recognized tribes in North Carolina. They are also known as the "People of the Fallen Star." They are considered to be Waccamaw, as are the Waccamaw Indian People, a state-recognized tribe from South Carolina. However, the two are not affiliated with each other. About 150 years later, the Englishman William Hilton recorded his encounter with ancestors of the Waccamaw Siouan people, calling them the Woccon. While the Waccamaw were never populous, the arrival of settlers and their diseases in the 16th century resulted in devastating population loss and dispersal.
Waccamaw (Woccon).culture: The Waccamaw's relationship with European settlers was based on trade (including guns and enslaved people), alliances, aid, and friendship, yet was all the while marred by European-borne disease, displacement due to European settlement, hostility, and outright warfare. edentary villages have appeared in the area since at least 3,000-500 B.C., with maize being the staple crop of the area. Chiefdoms arose in the area from 1150-1200 A.D.

Warraskoyack.description: The Warraskoyack, also spelled Warraskoyak or Warrosquyoake, were an Indigenous people whose homeland was in present-day Isle of Wight County, Virginia. Their main village was located on the Pagan River near present-day Smithfield, and John Smith also mapped two other towns, Mokete and Mathomauk, along the south bank of the James River. The Warraskoyack traded with English colonists soon after Jamestown was founded and at first maintained a relatively peaceful relationship with them. That relationship deteriorated as English settlers moved into Warraskoyack lands, destroyed cornfields and claimed riverfront areas for plantations. By the 1620s, warfare and colonial expansion had forced the Warraskoyack from much of their homeland.
Warraskoyack.language: The Warraskoyack language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of tidewater Virginia. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Warraskoyack.culture: Warraskoyack culture was shaped by the James River, Pagan River, wetlands, forests and nearby swamps. Their homeland gave them access to oysters, fish, hunting grounds, gathering areas and canoe routes for trade and travel. They lived in villages that likely included longhouses made from poles covered with bark or reed matting, similar to other Native towns in the region. The Warraskoyack traded food with the English for metal tools and prestige goods, and they also helped guide English searches for survivors of the Roanoke Colony. Their history reflects both early diplomacy with Jamestown and the severe disruption caused by plantation expansion, warfare and the replacement of Native place names with English ones.

Wateree.description: The Wateree were an Indigenous people of the interior Carolinas. Spanish explorers first encountered them in 1567 in western North Carolina, but by the early 1700s they had moved southeast into what is now South Carolina. They settled along the Wateree River, near present-day Camden, South Carolina. Once a larger tribe, the Wateree suffered severe population loss during the Yamasee War of 1715 and later merged with neighboring Native peoples, especially the Catawba.
Wateree.language: The Wateree likely spoke a Siouan-Catawba language related to Catawba and other Piedmont Siouan languages. Their language was still distinct enough to be noted in the eighteenth century, but it was not fully preserved in written records.
Wateree.culture: The Wateree lived along river systems that supported farming, hunting, fishing and trade. Their location near the Wateree River connected them to other Native communities of the Carolina Piedmont and interior Southeast. Historical records suggest they maintained a distinct identity into the mid-1700s, including land transactions involving Wateree people. Although the Wateree no longer exist as a separate tribe, some descendants were likely absorbed into the Catawba Nation and other Native communities of the region.

Waxhaw.description: Long before European explorers came to the Americas, the Native American, known as the Waxhaws, inhabited the area near Cane Creek in the Catawba River Valley. The origin of their name is not known for sure, but it may mean People of the Cane, in the common trade language used by native groups. By 1720, most of the Waxhaws had been wiped out by the dreaded diseases brought over by European settlers, particularly smallpox. The rest were decimated during the Yamasee Wars, which pitted them against South Carolina colonists and larger, more powerful, tribes.
Waxhaw.culture: The Waxhaws were referred to as “flatheads” by early explorers and other natives. This nickname no doubt came from the tribes harmless practice of placing small sandbags on the foreheads of infants, resulting in wide-set eyes and a reshaped, flat forehead. The typical Waxhaw dwellings were similar to those of other peoples of the region. They were covered in bark. Ceremonial buildings, however, were usually thatched with reeds and bullgrass. The people held ceremonial dances, tribal meetings, and other important rites in these council houses.

Werowocomoco.description: Werowocomoco was not a single tribe, but an important political, spiritual and ceremonial center of the Powhatan world. Located in present-day Gloucester County, Virginia, it served as a major seat of power for Wahunsunacock, the paramount chief known to the English as Powhatan. The name Werowocomoco is often translated as “place of leadership,” reflecting its role as a center of authority and diplomacy. It was also the place where Powhatan, Captain John Smith and Pocahontas became connected in early English accounts. After Powhatan moved westward around 1609 to distance his people from the English, the site faded from colonial records until archaeologists confirmed its location in the early 2000s.
Werowocomoco.language: The people connected to Werowocomoco spoke a language in the Algonquian language family, related to other Native languages of tidewater Virginia. The name Werowocomoco comes from this language tradition.
Werowocomoco.culture: Werowocomoco was a place of leadership, ceremony, diplomacy, trade and spiritual life. Long before the English arrived at Jamestown, the site had been important to Native communities for thousands of years. Leaders and visitors came there for political meetings, tribute relationships and ceremonial gatherings. Its location near waterways connected it to other towns across the Chesapeake region. Today, Werowocomoco is recognized as a sacred Indigenous place and is protected as part of the Captain John Smith Chesapeake National Historic Trail.

Westo.description: The Westo lived along the Savannah River near present-day Augusta, Georgia, from about 1660 to 1680. They became one of the most powerful Native groups in the Southeast during the late seventeenth century because of their early access to English firearms through trade with Virginia. After moving into the Savannah River region, the Westo used their military power to raid neighboring tribes and disrupt Spanish mission communities in Guale and Mocama. By the 1680s, conflicts with other Native groups and colonial alliances led to the destruction of the Westo as a distinct tribe.
Westo.language: The Westo spoke an Iroquoian language, though their specific language was not well recorded. Their Iroquoian background likely connected them to peoples originally from farther north before their movement into the Southeast.
Westo.culture: The Westo were known for their role in the colonial-era trade of guns, goods and enslaved Native captives. They traded captives to English colonists in exchange for firearms, ammunition, metal tools, blankets and glass beads. Their access to guns gave them a major military advantage over neighboring groups who still relied mainly on bows and arrows. This power made the Westo feared throughout parts of Georgia, Florida and the Carolinas, but it also drew them into violent conflicts that eventually led to their decline.

Weyanock.description: The Weyanock, also spelled Weyanoke, were an Indigenous people whose homeland was along the middle stretches of the James River in present-day Virginia. Their territory included land on both sides of the river, with a main town near Weyanoke Point in Charles City County and another settlement in present-day Prince George County. In the early 1600s, they numbered about 500 people and were governed by their own chief while also connected to Powhatan’s political world. Their riverfront homeland was highly valued, and English expansion placed increasing pressure on their towns and farmland. After conflict, displacement and attacks from other Native groups in the late 1600s, many Weyanock people moved south into present-day North Carolina and merged with neighboring Native communities.
Weyanock.language: The Weyanock language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of tidewater Virginia. Their specific language was not preserved in detail.
Weyanock.culture: Weyanock culture was closely tied to the James River and its surrounding creeks, which served as major routes for travel, trade and communication. They used canoes to move between settlements and relied on both sides of the river as part of their homeland. Their communities farmed crops such as corn, beans and squash, while also fishing, hunting and gathering seasonal foods. The Weyanock signed the Treaty of Middle Plantation in 1677, but continued colonial pressure and regional conflict pushed many survivors away from their ancestral lands. Their descendants likely became part of other Native communities, including groups connected to the Nottoway and Tuscarora.

Wicocomico.description: The Wicocomico, also spelled Wiccocomico, are an Indigenous people whose historic homeland was in Northumberland County, Virginia, along the Chesapeake Bay and nearby waterways. They were among the first Native people on the mainland to encounter Captain John Smith during his 1608 exploration of the Chesapeake region. In 1656, colonial authorities set aside a 4,400-acre reservation for the Wicocomico and related Native communities at Dividing Creek, but English encroachment and court disputes continued to threaten their land. In 1710, William Taptico presented three arrows to the Virginia Assembly as a symbol of the tribe’s standing and land claims. After the death of King William Taptico Jr. in 1719, colonial authorities treated Wicocomico land claims as extinguished, though descendants continued to remain in and near their homelands.
Wicocomico.language: The Wicocomico language was part of the Algonquian language family and was connected to other Native languages of the Chesapeake region. The modern Wicocomico community has identified ancestral language revival as part of its cultural preservation work.
Wicocomico.culture: Wicocomico culture is rooted in the waterways, forests and coastal lands of Virginia’s Northern Neck. Their community relied on fishing, farming, hunting, gathering and trade, while maintaining leadership through figures such as Pekwem, Machywap and the Taptico family. Colonial records show repeated struggles over land, including disputes with settlers who encroached on Wicocomico territory. In the twentieth century, racial reclassification and “paper genocide” further threatened Virginia Native identity, but Wicocomico descendants continued to preserve family histories and community memory. Today, the Wicocomico Indian Nation works to document genealogy, revive language, correct historical misconceptions, protect the environment and reclaim its cultural rights.

Winyah (Winyaw).description: The Winyaw were a Native American tribe living near Winyah Bay, Black River, and the lower course of the Pee Dee River in South Carolina. The Winyaw people disappeared as a distinct entity after 1720 and are thought to have merged with the Waccamaw. The meaning of the name Winyaw is unknown. Winyaw has also been written as Winyah, Weenee, and Wineaw. The Winyaw were first mentioned by colonists of South Carolina after 1670. The tribe at first allied with the English colonists who settled in Charles Town, but this friendship soon was shattered when European slave dealers instigated a war against them in 1683 as an excuse to capture slaves.
Winyah (Winyaw).culture: They located their villages along waterways, practiced riverine agriculture, and supplemented their diet by hunting, fishing, and gathering. Gender roles were divided to provide the sense of balance required by the southeastern cosmology and social ethic. Women were responsible for the vegetable diet and performed most of the agricultural work; men provided meat and therefore spent much of their energy in hunting. Women also fulfilled domestic responsibilities, while men engaged in war and games.

WiYaPeMiAk (Weapemeoc-Yeopim).language: The WiYaPeMiAk spoke an Eastern Algonquian language related to other coastal Native languages of North Carolina and Virginia. Many original words were not preserved, but place names such as Yeopim, Pasquotank, Perquimans and Currituck remain connected to their history.
WiYaPeMiAk (Weapemeoc-Yeopim).culture: The Weapemeoc-Yeopim lived in a coastal and river-based environment. They fished, hunted, gathered wild foods and farmed crops such as corn, beans and squash. The Great Dismal Swamp was especially important as a source of food, materials and refuge. Community identity continued through family networks, churches, places like Shiloh and Indiantown, and modern efforts to reclaim Weapemeoc-Yeopim heritage.

Yamasee.description: The Yamasees (also spelled Yamassees, Yemasees or Yemassees) were a multiethnic confederation of Native Americans who lived in the coastal region of present-day northern coastal Georgia near the Savannah River and later in northeastern Florida. The Yamasees engaged in revolts and wars with other native groups and Europeans living in North America, specifically from Florida to North Carolina. After the Yamasees migrated to the Carolinas, they began participating in the Indian slave trade in the American Southeast. They raided other tribes to take captives for sale to European colonists.
Yamasee.culture: The Yemassee Tribe canoes and homes are made out of palmetto fronds. Oyster shells for spears and knives and animal skins to wear as clothing. Animal skins and bark makes a great structure for wigwams. Sea grass also makes clothing and baskets. The Yemassee Tribe had fished in the Savannah River and the coastlines of South Carolina and Florida for the native fish in the Savannah River and along the coast lines in both South Carolina and Florida. The Yemassee also hunted deer and bears and they used all parts of the animals that they killed.

Yazoo.description: The Yazoo were a small Indigenous tribe historically located along the Yazoo River in present-day Mississippi. They were closely connected to other Tunica-speaking peoples, including the Tunica, Koroa and possibly the Tioux. In 1729, the Yazoo and Koroa attacked and destroyed the French Fort St. Peter on the lower Yazoo River. After conflict with the French and pressure from neighboring groups, the Yazoo declined as a separate tribe and were likely absorbed into related Native communities.
Yazoo.language: The Yazoo language was not well recorded, but it is believed to have been related to Tunica, a language isolate. Their close ties with the Tunica and Koroa suggest they belonged to the same lower Mississippi Valley language and cultural world.
Yazoo.culture: The Yazoo lived in the fertile riverlands of the Mississippi Delta, where farming, fishing, hunting and gathering supported daily life. Like other Native peoples of the lower Mississippi Valley, they likely grew crops such as corn, beans, squash, tobacco and sunflowers, while also gathering nuts, seeds and fruits. The Yazoo River connected them to trade, travel and neighboring communities. Their history reflects both the strength of Mississippi River Indigenous societies and the disruption caused by colonial warfare, disease and shifting alliances.

Yeopim.description:
The Yeopim were an Indigenous people of northeastern North Carolina. They were part of the Algonquian-speaking peoples of the Carolina coast and lived in what are now Camden, Currituck, Perquimans and Pasquotank counties. They were closely related to other Native communities in the region, including the Weapemeoc. Although the Yeopim were small in number, they played an important role in early North Carolina history because they were among the first Native peoples to encounter English explorers and settlers in the region.

In 1584, Captain Philip Amadas encountered Yeopim and other Weapemeoc people while scouting possible locations for the first Roanoke colony. During the seventeenth century, English settlement expanded into Yeopim homelands. In 1660, Kiscutanewh, the King of the Yeopim, granted land to Nathaniel Batts. In 1662, Yeopim leaders granted another tract to George Durant. These became some of the earliest recorded land deeds in North Carolina history.

As more English settlers moved into the region, the Yeopim faced increasing pressure on their land. By 1697, they complained to the colonial government about settler encroachment. In 1704, North Carolina’s Executive Council created a 10,240-acre reservation for the Yeopim on the North River. The deed recognized the Yeopim king and his nation’s hunting and fishing rights, while requiring a yearly quitrent of one ear of corn. Over time, Yeopim landholdings declined. In 1723, they sold 640 acres, and in 1739 they received permission to sell additional land as they wished. The village of Yawpim appeared on the 1733 Moseley map. That site is now known as Indiantown and was studied by East Carolina University archaeologists in 1984. By the mid-eighteenth century, the Yeopim had declined greatly in number and no longer existed as a major separate tribal community in North Carolina.
Yeopim.language: The Yeopim spoke an Algonquian language related to the languages of other coastal Carolina Native peoples, including the Weapemeoc. Their language belonged to the same broad language family as many Native peoples of the Atlantic coast. Very little of the Yeopim language was recorded before the tribe’s population declined, so most of what is known about their language comes from their connection to neighboring Algonquian-speaking communities.
Yeopim.culture:
The Yeopim lived in the river, sound and coastal environments of northeastern North Carolina. Their homeland included waterways, forests, marshes and fertile land that supported fishing, hunting, gathering and farming. Like other Algonquian-speaking peoples of the Carolina coast, they likely grew crops such as corn, beans and squash while also gathering wild foods and fishing in rivers, creeks and sounds. The North River and nearby waterways were important for food, travel and trade.

Yeopim political life included local leadership under a king, as shown by records naming Kiscutanewh as King of the Yeopim. Their communities were connected to neighboring Native peoples through language, trade and shared coastal lifeways. Colonial land pressure, disease, population loss and the sale of reservation land weakened the Yeopim community over time. Although the Yeopim disappeared from colonial records as a distinct tribe by the mid-1700s, their history remains important to the Indigenous history of northeastern North Carolina and to places such as Indiantown, where their village was remembered on early maps.

Yesan (Tutelo).description:
The Yesan, more commonly known in historical records as the Tutelo, were a Native American people whose homelands were in the interior Southeast, especially above the Fall Line in present-day Virginia, West Virginia and North Carolina. They were part of the broader group of Siouan-speaking peoples of the Virginia Piedmont and were related in language and culture to neighboring groups such as the Monacan, Manahoac and Saponi. English records from 1671 mention a Tutelo town near present-day Salem, Virginia, showing that the tribe was already known to colonial explorers by the late seventeenth century.

The Tutelo lived during a period of intense pressure from English colonists, the expanding fur trade, disease and warfare. During the Beaver Wars, the Iroquois Confederacy pushed southward and displaced many Native communities in the Ohio Valley and Virginia interior. To survive, the Tutelo joined with other Virginia Siouan peoples, including the Saponi and Occaneechi. Together, they were sometimes known collectively as the "Nahyssan" or later as the "Saponi-Tutelo". By the early eighteenth century, they had moved through several locations, including islands near the place where the Dan and Staunton rivers join to form the Roanoke River, the headwaters of the Yadkin River in North Carolina and Fort Christanna in Brunswick County, Virginia.

By the 1730s and 1740s, many Tutelo and allied Siouan people moved north to Pennsylvania and then into western New York, seeking protection from the Haudenosaunee, also known as the Iroquois Confederacy. In 1753, the Tutelo were admitted into the Iroquois League and became closely connected with the Cayuga. They later lived at Coreorgonel, a town near Cayuga Inlet in present-day New York. That town was destroyed in 1779 during the Sullivan Expedition of the American Revolutionary War. Afterward, many Tutelo moved with the Cayuga to Canada, especially to what is now the Six Nations of the Grand River Reserve in Ontario. Over time, many Tutelo descendants intermarried with Cayuga and other Haudenosaunee families, while other descendants are connected to Native communities in North Carolina, including Saponi-descended communities.
Yesan (Tutelo).language: The Yesan, or Tutelo, spoke a Siouan language known as Tutelo. Their language was related to the languages of neighboring Siouan-speaking peoples such as the Monacan, Manahoac and Saponi. The name "Yesan" is a Tutelo name for the people themselves. Over time, as the Tutelo merged with other Native communities and moved northward, the language declined. During the nineteenth century, Smithsonian researchers and other scholars collected Tutelo linguistic material. The last known fluent speaker of the Tutelo language died in 1898, but recorded vocabulary and language materials remain important evidence of the tribe’s identity and history.
Yesan (Tutelo).culture:
The Tutelo lived in the river valleys, forests and mountain regions of the interior Southeast. They were familiar with the Appalachian Mountains and the Ohio, Roanoke, Dan, Staunton and Yadkin river systems. These landscapes supported hunting, fishing, farming and gathering. The Tutelo hunted game, fished in rivers and streams and farmed crops such as corn, beans and squash. Their agricultural knowledge helped provide a stable food supply, while seasonal hunting and fishing connected them to a wider landscape of mountains, valleys and waterways.

Tutelo communities were organized through clans, local leaders and councils that made political and social decisions. Although not much is known about the full details of their social organization, historical accounts suggest that leadership was not strictly inherited by one family line. The Tutelo were also skilled makers of tools, pottery, beadwork and other items used in daily life and cultural expression. These objects were practical, but they also reflected community identity, beliefs and traditions.

As disease, warfare and colonial expansion reduced their population, Tutelo culture continued through alliance and adaptation. Their movement from Virginia and North Carolina to Pennsylvania, New York and Canada reflects both displacement and survival. Although the Tutelo no longer exist as a separate federally recognized tribe, their descendants continue through Native communities connected to the Cayuga, Six Nations of the Grand River and Saponi-descended peoples in North Carolina.

Youghtanund.description:
The Youghtanund, also recorded as "Youghtamund", were an Indigenous people associated with the Powhatan chiefdom of early seventeenth-century Virginia. They were one of the smaller and less-documented communities within the Powhatan political world. The attached history describes them as one of the early constituent groups of the Powhatan confederacy and notes that they were closely connected to the Pamunkey and Manskin peoples. Early map evidence places the Youghtanund near “Island Field,” also known as Guttins Island, and near the Pamunkey River region. 

The Powhatan chiefdom occupied much of eastern Virginia, from the James River north toward the Potomac River and parts of the Eastern Shore. The Youghtanund appear to have lived northwest of the Pamunkey, along the Pamunkey River toward the area where the North Anna and South Anna rivers meet to form the Pamunkey River.

In 1607 and 1608, English accounts identified "Pomiscatuckas" the weroance, or leader, of the Youghtanund. Captain John Smith listed the Youghtanund among the Powhatan-allied communities and counted about thirty Youghtanund warriors, though early English estimates were often incomplete or inconsistent. The tribe’s recorded history becomes harder to trace after the Anglo-Powhatan wars. Following the Second Anglo-Powhatan War and the English victory in the 1640s, the Youghtanund name disappears from many records. The attached source suggests that the Youghtanund may have been absorbed into or closely merged with the Manskin, with surviving people relocating or being incorporated into other Native communities. By the eighteenth century, the Youghtanund were no longer documented as a separate tribal community.
Youghtanund.language: The Youghtanund spoke an Eastern Algonquian language related to the languages of other Powhatan peoples. These related languages connected Native communities across tidewater Virginia through trade, diplomacy, kinship and political alliances. The Powhatan language was not fully preserved in written records, and very little specific Youghtanund vocabulary was recorded. Most of what is known about their language comes from their association with the wider Powhatan world, where Algonquian-speaking communities shared many cultural and linguistic ties.
Youghtanund.culture:
The Youghtanund lived within the river-centered world of the Powhatan peoples. Their communities were connected to the Pamunkey River and nearby waterways, which supported fishing, travel, trade and communication with neighboring towns. Like other Powhatan communities, they likely farmed corn, beans and squash; gathered wild plants and nuts; hunted deer and smaller animals; and fished in rivers and creeks. Powhatan people generally lived in settled villages with houses made from bent saplings covered with bark or reed mats, and their communities were organized around kinship, seasonal food cycles and local leadership.

The Youghtanund were governed by a local leader under the broader authority of Powhatan’s paramount chiefdom. Their leader Pomiscatuck is described in the attached history as an explorer and navigator, suggesting the importance of travel, river knowledge and diplomacy in Youghtanund life. As colonial warfare and English expansion disrupted Native communities in Virginia, the Youghtanund lost visibility as a distinct recorded group. Their history survives mainly through early maps, English accounts and their connection to neighboring Powhatan peoples such as the Pamunkey and Manskin.

ᏣᎳᎫᏪᏘᏱ Tsalaguwetiyi (Cherokee, East).description: The Cherokee are one of the Indigenous peoples of the Southeastern Woodlands of the United States. Prior to the 18th century, they were concentrated in their homelands, in towns along river valleys of what is now southwestern North Carolina, southeastern Tennessee, southwestern Virginia, edges of western South Carolina, northern Georgia and northeastern Alabama. In the 19th century, James Mooney, an early American ethnographer, recorded one oral tradition that told of the tribe having migrated south in ancient times from the Great Lakes region, where other Iroquoian peoples have been based. The first contact between Cherokees and Europeans was in 1540, when Hernando de Soto and several hundred of his conquistadors traveled through Cherokee territory during their expedition in what is now the southeastern United States.
ᏣᎳᎫᏪᏘᏱ Tsalaguwetiyi (Cherokee, East).language: Cherokee is an Iroquoian language, and the only Southern Iroquoian language spoken today.
ᏣᎳᎫᏪᏘᏱ Tsalaguwetiyi (Cherokee, East).culture: Much of what is known about pre-18th century Native American cultures has come from records of Spanish expeditions. The earliest ones of the mid-16th century encountered peoples of the Mississippian culture era, who were ancestral to tribes that emerged in the Southeast, such as the Cherokee, Muscogee, Cheraw, and Catawba. The Cherokee nation was composed of a confederacy. Cherokees wove baskets, made pottery, and cultivated corn (maize), beans, and squash. Deer, bear, and elk furnished meat and clothing. An important religious observance was the Busk, or Green Corn, festival, a firstfruits and new-fires celebration. The Spanish recorded a Chalaque people as living around the Keowee River, where western North Carolina, South Carolina, and northeastern Georgia meet. The Cherokee consider this area to be part of their homelands, which also extended into southeastern Tennessee.

𐓏𐒰𐓓𐒰𐓓𐒷 𐒼𐓂𐓊𐒻 𐓆𐒻𐒿𐒷 𐓀𐒰^𐓓𐒰^(Osage).description:
The 𐓏𐒰𐓓𐒰𐓓𐒷, commonly known as the Osage, are a Native nation whose ancestral history reaches across the Ohio Valley and the central plains. Osage oral history and historical research connect their earlier homelands to lands east of the Mississippi River, including parts of the Ohio Valley. Over time, the Osage moved west of the Mississippi and became strongly associated with present-day Missouri, Arkansas, Kansas and Oklahoma. By the early nineteenth century, Osage land primarily included parts of Missouri, Kansas, Oklahoma and Arkansas. 

Through treaties in 1808, 1818 and 1825, the Osage were pressured to give up large portions of their land. In 1839, during the same era as the Cherokee removal to Indian Territory, the U.S. government forced the Osage to leave parts of their homeland in what is now Oklahoma and move to Kansas. By the 1870s, many Osage had moved again, this time to land in present-day Osage County, Oklahoma. Unlike many tribes who were assigned land by the United States, the Osage purchased their reservation land, a decision that later became especially important when oil was discovered there. 

In the early twentieth century, the Osage negotiated to retain collective mineral rights beneath their reservation. When oil was discovered, Osage headrights provided tribal citizens with shares of mineral income. This wealth attracted intense outside pressure, fraud and violence. During the 1920s, a period remembered as the Reign of Terror, Osage people were targeted for their oil wealth through murder, theft and exploitation. Many deaths remain unresolved.  The Bureau of Indian Affairs notes that the Osage retained mineral rights in Osage County when the surface land was allotted in 1906.

The Osage also endured federal boarding school policies that tried to suppress their language, culture and traditions. Children were punished for speaking Osage and pressured to assimilate into English-speaking society. Despite these hardships, Osage families kept their identity, language, ceremonies and values alive. Today, the Osage Nation** is a federally recognized Native government headquartered in Pawhuska, Oklahoma. Its citizens continue to strengthen Osage sovereignty, protect cultural knowledge and revitalize the Osage language for future generations.
𐓏𐒰𐓓𐒰𐓓𐒷 𐒼𐓂𐓊𐒻 𐓆𐒻𐒿𐒷 𐓀𐒰^𐓓𐒰^(Osage).language:
The Osage language is a Dhegiha Siouan language related to the languages of the Omaha, Ponca, Kaw and Quapaw peoples. The Osage name for the people is written in the Osage script as "𐓏𐒰𐓓𐒰𐓓𐒷". The language remained strong in Osage homes for many generations, but boarding schools and assimilation policies severely disrupted its transmission. Osage children born in the early twentieth century were often sent away to schools where they were forced to speak English instead of Osage. Later generations grew up hearing Osage spoken by elders but often learned English as their first language. 

The Osage Nation has worked to reclaim and revitalize the language. In 2003, the Osage Language Program was created to support language learning and preservation. The program has developed classes, learning materials and new teaching methods for tribal citizens. The Osage Nation Language Department describes its mission as revitalizing the Osage language and teaching Osage people to use it in daily conversation.
