Today, Osage language classes are offered in person and online, with beginner through advanced options for Osage citizens.
𐓏𐒰𐓓𐒰𐓓𐒷 𐒼𐓂𐓊𐒻 𐓆𐒻𐒿𐒷 𐓀𐒰^𐓓𐒰^(Osage).culture:
Osage culture is rooted in relationships among people, land, language, ceremony and balance. The Osage traditionally moved with the seasons, traveling between village homes, hunting grounds and river valleys. Their earlier homelands connected the woodlands of the Ohio Valley with the plains west of the Mississippi River. As they lived in Missouri, Kansas, Arkansas and Oklahoma, Osage people developed deep knowledge of prairies, rivers, forests, plants, animals and seasonal cycles. 

Osage families farmed, hunted and gathered to support their communities. They grew crops such as corn, beans and squash, while also hunting bison, deer and other game. Social and ceremonial life emphasized balance, responsibility and respect for the natural world. The attached history describes an Osage understanding that there is balance and imbalance in the universe, and that when things go wrong, balance must eventually be restored. 

Today, Osage culture continues through language revitalization, traditional prayer, community gatherings, dances, art, oral history, education and the work of the Osage Nation government. The Nation’s cultural preservation vision includes a flourishing Osage culture, a revitalized language and Osage history taught to future generations. Despite removal, allotment, the Reign of Terror and boarding school violence, the Osage Nation remains a living sovereign nation centered in Osage County, Oklahoma.

Jeaga.description: At the time of the Spanish discovery of Florida, the Jeaga (“hay-gwa”) lived along Florida’s east coast in the modern St. Lucie County area. They utilized the rich coastal resources as well as the flatlands inland from the coast for 10 -20 miles, possibly further. The Jaega (also Jega, Xega, Geiga) were Native Americans living in a chiefdom of the same name, which included the coastal parts of present-day Martin County and northern Palm Beach County, Florida at the time of initial European contact, and until the 18th century. The name Jobe, or Jove, has been identified as a synonym of Jaega, a sub-group of the Jaega, or a town of the Jaega. Throughout the 16th and 17th centuries, the Jeaga, particularly those in Jobe, gained power and influence by salvaging materials from ships wrecked along the coast of Palm Beach County.
Jeaga.language: The Ais and Jaega languages have been tentatively assigned by some authors to the Muskogean language family, and by others to the Arawakan language family.
Jeaga.culture: The Jeagas foraged for coco plums, sea grapes and palm berries, They also drank a frothy, ceremonial tea made from the roasted leaves of the cassina plant. Known as the "black drink", it was high in caffeine and obviously made quite an impression on the Europeans. The Jeaga were masters at carving dugout canoes from cypress trees and expertly navigated Florida’s waterways to Lake Okeechobee and throughout South Florida to trade with other tribes. Sharks, manatees, deer, squirrel, sea turtles, mullet, snapper, sea grapes, coco plums, and saw palmetto berries were some of nature’s resources that fed the early people. They lived in wigwam-style homes consisting of wooden poles stuck into the ground and covered with palmetto leaves.

Arrohateck.description:
The 𐓏𐒰𐓓𐒰𐓓𐒷, commonly known as the Osage, are a Native nation whose ancestral history reaches across the Ohio Valley and the central plains. Osage oral history and historical research connect their earlier homelands to lands east of the Mississippi River, including parts of the Ohio Valley. Over time, the Osage moved west of the Mississippi and became strongly associated with present-day Missouri, Arkansas, Kansas and Oklahoma. By the early nineteenth century, Osage land primarily included parts of Missouri, Kansas, Oklahoma and Arkansas. 

Through treaties in 1808, 1818 and 1825, the Osage were pressured to give up large portions of their land. In 1839, during the same era as the Cherokee removal to Indian Territory, the U.S. government forced the Osage to leave parts of their homeland in what is now Oklahoma and move to Kansas. By the 1870s, many Osage had moved again, this time to land in present-day Osage County, Oklahoma. Unlike many tribes who were assigned land by the United States, the Osage purchased their reservation land, a decision that later became especially important when oil was discovered there. 

In the early twentieth century, the Osage negotiated to retain collective mineral rights beneath their reservation. When oil was discovered, Osage headrights provided tribal citizens with shares of mineral income. This wealth attracted intense outside pressure, fraud and violence. During the 1920s, a period remembered as the Reign of Terror, Osage people were targeted for their oil wealth through murder, theft and exploitation. Many deaths remain unresolved.  The Bureau of Indian Affairs notes that the Osage retained mineral rights in Osage County when the surface land was allotted in 1906.

The Osage also endured federal boarding school policies that tried to suppress their language, culture and traditions. Children were punished for speaking Osage and pressured to assimilate into English-speaking society. Despite these hardships, Osage families kept their identity, language, ceremonies and values alive. Today, the Osage Nation** is a federally recognized Native government headquartered in Pawhuska, Oklahoma. Its citizens continue to strengthen Osage sovereignty, protect cultural knowledge and revitalize the Osage language for future generations.
Arrohateck.language:
The Osage language is a Dhegiha Siouan language related to the languages of the Omaha, Ponca, Kaw and Quapaw peoples. The Osage name for the people is written in the Osage script as "𐓏𐒰𐓓𐒰𐓓𐒷". The language remained strong in Osage homes for many generations, but boarding schools and assimilation policies severely disrupted its transmission. Osage children born in the early twentieth century were often sent away to schools where they were forced to speak English instead of Osage. Later generations grew up hearing Osage spoken by elders but often learned English as their first language. 

The Osage Nation has worked to reclaim and revitalize the language. In 2003, the Osage Language Program was created to support language learning and preservation. The program has developed classes, learning materials and new teaching methods for tribal citizens. The Osage Nation Language Department describes its mission as revitalizing the Osage language and teaching Osage people to use it in daily conversation. Today, Osage language classes are offered in person and online, with beginner through advanced options for Osage citizens.
Arrohateck.culture:
Osage culture is rooted in relationships among people, land, language, ceremony and balance. The Osage traditionally moved with the seasons, traveling between village homes, hunting grounds and river valleys. Their earlier homelands connected the woodlands of the Ohio Valley with the plains west of the Mississippi River. As they lived in Missouri, Kansas, Arkansas and Oklahoma, Osage people developed deep knowledge of prairies, rivers, forests, plants, animals and seasonal cycles. 

Osage families farmed, hunted and gathered to support their communities. They grew crops such as corn, beans and squash, while also hunting bison, deer and other game. Social and ceremonial life emphasized balance, responsibility and respect for the natural world. The attached history describes an Osage understanding that there is balance and imbalance in the universe, and that when things go wrong, balance must eventually be restored. 

Today, Osage culture continues through language revitalization, traditional prayer, community gatherings, dances, art, oral history, education and the work of the Osage Nation government. The Nation’s cultural preservation vision includes a flourishing Osage culture, a revitalized language and Osage history taught to future generations. Despite removal, allotment, the Reign of Terror and boarding school violence, the Osage Nation remains a living sovereign nation centered in Osage County, Oklahoma.

Winyah.description: The Winyaw were a Native American tribe living near Winyah Bay, Black River, and the lower course of the Pee Dee River in South Carolina. The Winyaw people disappeared as a distinct entity after 1720 and are thought to have merged with the Waccamaw. The meaning of the name Winyaw is unknown. Winyaw has also been written as Winyah, Weenee, and Wineaw. The Winyaw were first mentioned by colonists of South Carolina after 1670. The tribe at first allied with the English colonists who settled in Charles Town, but this friendship soon was shattered when European slave dealers instigated a war against them in 1683 as an excuse to capture slaves.
Winyah.culture: They located their villages along waterways, practiced riverine agriculture, and supplemented their diet by hunting, fishing, and gathering. Gender roles were divided to provide the sense of balance required by the southeastern cosmology and social ethic. Women were responsible for the vegetable diet and performed most of the agricultural work; men provided meat and therefore spent much of their energy in hunting. Women also fulfilled domestic responsibilities, while men engaged in war and games.
